Claude can accurately replicate transaction handling, error formats, and logging conventions without relying
on abstraction or interpretation.
A. Describe the patterns from the three modules in natural language in your prompt, explaining the
transaction handling approach, error format, and logging conventions Claude should follow. ❌ (Incorrect)
Natural language descriptions are prone to ambiguity and may miss subtle implementation details present in
the actual code.
C. Ask Claude to explore your codebase to find and understand the transaction, error handling, and logging
patterns before generating the new module. ❌ (Incorrect)
While exploration helps, it is less precise and less reliable than explicitly providing the canonical examples.
D. Add documentation of each pattern to your CLAUDE.md file, establishing them as project conventions
that Claude will apply automatically. ❌ (Incorrect)
This is unnecessary overhead for a one-off task and does not guarantee accurate replication of nuanced
implementation details.

question: You've asked Claude to write a data migration script, but the initial output doesn't correctly handle records with null values in required fields. What's the most effective way to iterate toward a working solution?

choices.A: Provide a test case with example input containing null values and the expected output, then ask Claude to fix it.
choices.B: Manually edit the generated code to fix the null handling, then continue working with Claude on other parts.
choices.C: Describe the null value problem in detail and ask Claude to regenerate the entire script with improved edge case handling.
choices.D: Add "think harder about edge cases" to your prompt and request a complete rewrite of the migration logic.

explanation:
A. Provide a test case with example input containing null values and the expected output, then ask Claude
to fix it. ✅ (Correct)
The most effective iterative approach is to make the failure explicit and verifiable. By providing concrete
input/output examples for null-handling edge cases, you give a precise contract for the expected behavior.
This allows Claude to adjust the implementation accurately and testably, rather than relying on vague
descriptions or full rewrites.
B. Manually edit the generated code to fix the null handling, then continue working with Claude on other
parts. ❌ (Incorrect)
Manual fixes break the iterative feedback loop and make it harder for Claude to learn from structured
correction signals.
C. Describe the null value problem in detail and ask Claude to regenerate the entire script with improved
edge case handling. ❌ (Incorrect)
Regenerating the full script is inefficient and risks introducing regressions in already correct parts of the
logic.
D. Add "think harder about edge cases" to your prompt and request a complete rewrite of the migration
logic. ❌ (Incorrect)
This is too vague and does not provide actionable guidance or measurable correctness criteria.

question: You've documented API error handling conventions in a CLAUDE.md file at your project root, specifying that endpoint handlers should use a custom ApiError class. After several sessions, you notice Claude Code sometimes follows these conventions and sometimes uses generic try/catch blocks with string messages. The inconsistency appears random across different coding sessions. What's the most efficient first diagnostic step?

choices.A: Add more detailed code examples to your CLAUDE.md showing the exact ApiError usage pattern for different endpoint types.
choices.B: Run /memory to check which memory files are loaded and verify your CLAUDE.md is included.
choices.C: Search for conflicting instructions in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md or ~/.claude/rules/ that might override your project conventions.
choices.D: Create path-specific rules in claude/rules/handlers.md with YAML frontmatter scoping the error handling instructions to your API handler files.

explanation:
B. Run /memory to check which memory files are loaded and verify your CLAUDE.md is included. ✅
(Correct)
The first and most efficient diagnostic step is to confirm whether the expected project-level context is
actually being loaded. Since behavior is inconsistent across sessions, the most likely issue is not the rule
content itself but context loading or memory inclusion. Checking /memory verifies whether CLAUDE.md and
other relevant rules are being applied in the current session.
A. Add more detailed code examples to your CLAUDE.md showing the exact ApiError usage pattern for
different endpoint types. ❌ (Incorrect)
This addresses rule clarity, but the issue is not necessarily lack of detail—it may be missing or conflicting
context.
C. Search for conflicting instructions in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md or ~/.claude/rules/ that might override your
project conventions. ❌ (Incorrect)
This is a valid next step, but premature before confirming whether the project rules are even being loaded.
D. Create path-specific rules in claude/rules/handlers.md with YAML frontmatter scoping the error
handling instructions to your API handler files. ❌ (Incorrect)
This is a structural fix, not a diagnostic step. You should first identify why the inconsistency is happening.

question: Your monorepo contains shared coding standards in /docs/standards/security-rules.md (for services handling user data), testing-patterns.md (for all packages), and api-conventions.md (for API-facing services). Your 15 packages are organized by feature domain (/packages/auth/, /packages/billing/, /packages/notifications/, etc.) without naming conventions indicating which handle user data or expose APIs. Package maintainers are expected to configure their own local development settings, as they understand their package's domain requirements. Currently, all package CLAUDE.md files duplicate all three standards, applying irrelevant guidance. What's the most effective approach?

choices.A: Create claude/rules/ files for each standard with YAML frontmatter paths listing every package directory where that standard should apply.
choices.B: Create a shared-standards.nd that uses @imports to combine all three standards, then have each package's CLAUDE.md import that combined file.
choices.C: Put all standards in the root CLAUDE.md with override instructions like "ignore security-rules.md when working in packages that don't handle user data."
choices.D: Use @imports in each package's CLAUDE.md to reference only the specific standard files relevant to that package, based on the maintainer's domain knowledge.

explanation:
D. Use @imports in each package's CLAUDE.md to reference only the specific standard files relevant to
that package, based on the maintainer's domain knowledge. ✅ (Correct)
Since the package structure does not inherently indicate which standards apply, and maintainers are
expected to understand their package's requirements, the most effective approach is for each package's
CLAUDE.md to import only the relevant standards. This eliminates duplication, keeps guidance targeted, and
avoids loading irrelevant instructions while leveraging the maintainers' domain expertise.
A. Create .claude/rules/ files for each standard with YAML frontmatter paths listing every package
directory where that standard should apply. ❌ (Incorrect)
This requires centrally maintaining package-to-standard mappings, which is difficult when applicability
depends on domain knowledge rather than directory structure.
B. Create a shared-standards.md that uses @imports to combine all three standards, then have each
package's CLAUDE.md import that combined file. ❌ (Incorrect)
This continues loading irrelevant standards into every package, which is the problem being solved.
C. Put all standards in the root CLAUDE.md with override instructions like "ignore security-rules.md when
working in packages that don't handle user data." ❌ (Incorrect)
This increases context size and complexity, forcing Claude to process and ignore unnecessary guidance
rather than loading only what is needed.

question: The system processes product reviews using tool use with a defined schema: rating (integer 1-5), pros (string array), cons (string array), and overall_sentiment (enum: positive, negative, mixed). Testing reveals two issues with brief or ambiguous reviews (~20% of the dataset): (1) for reviews like "Great product!", Claude fabricates specific pros and cons rather than indicating this information isn't explicitly stated, and (2) for sarcastic reviews like "Well that was... interesting", Claude picks sentiment arbitrarily since there's no option for ambiguous cases. What schema modification best addresses both issues?

choices.A: Add an extraction_confidence field (0.0-1.0) for each value, and filter outputs where any confidence falls below a threshold.
choices.B: Allow null values for pros/cons, and add "unclear" to the sentiment enum.
choices.C: Allow empty arrays for pros/cons as valid output, and add "unclear" to the sentiment enum.
choices.D: Make pros and cons optional fields, and add "neutral" and "unclear" to the sentiment enum.

explanation:
C. Allow empty arrays for pros/cons as valid output, and add "unclear" to the sentiment enum. ✅ (Correct)
This directly addresses both problems:
For reviews like "Great product!", there may be no explicitly stated pros or cons. Allowing empty arrays lets
the model accurately represent that no specific pros or cons were mentioned, instead of hallucinating details.
For ambiguous or sarcastic reviews like "Well that was... interesting", adding "unclear" to the sentiment
enum provides a valid option when sentiment cannot be determined reliably from the text.
A. Add an extraction_confidence field (0.0-1.0) for each value, and filter outputs where any confidence falls
below a threshold. ❌ (Incorrect)
Confidence scores do not solve the underlying schema problem. The model may still fabricate pros/cons or
guess sentiment.
B. Allow null values for pros/cons, and add "unclear" to the sentiment enum. ❌ (Incorrect)
Null suggests the fields are missing rather than that no pros/cons were explicitly stated. Empty arrays more
accurately represent "none identified."
D. Make pros and cons optional fields, and add "neutral" and "unclear" to the sentiment enum. ❌
(Incorrect)
Optional fields can lead to inconsistent outputs. "Neutral" is different from "unclear" and does not address
ambiguity in determining sentiment.

question: After your daily batch of 10,000 documents completes, 300 documents (3%) failed with "context_length_exceeded" errors. The results file identifies each failure by custom_id. What's the most cost- effective approach to process these failures?

choices.A: Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled to reduce the cost of retrying requests with identical system prompts
choices.B: Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the partial extractions
choices.C: Increase the max_tokens parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit them in a new batch
choices.D: Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window

explanation:
B. Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the
partial extractions. ✅ (Correct)
The failures are specifically due to context_length_exceeded, meaning the documents are too large for the
model's context window. The most cost-effective solution is to retry only the failed 300 documents, split
them into smaller chunks that fit within context limits, and then merge the results. This avoids reprocessing
the 9,700 documents that already succeeded.
A. Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled to reduce the cost of retrying requests with
identical system prompts ❌ (Incorrect)
Prompt caching may reduce costs, but it does not solve the context-length issue and unnecessarily
reprocesses successful documents.
C. Increase the max_tokens parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit them in a new batch ❌
(Incorrect)
max_tokens controls output length, not input context capacity. It will not resolve context_length_exceeded
errors.
D. Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window ❌
(Incorrect)
This is significantly more expensive and unnecessary when only 3% of documents failed.

question: The system needs to extract candidate information (name, contact details, skills, work experience, education) from uploaded resumes. The extracted data must strictly conform to a predefined JSON schema, as missing required fields or incorrect data types will cause downstream validation failures. What is the most reliable approach to ensure Claude's output consistently matches the schema?

choices.A: Define a tool with an input schema matching your required JSON structure and extract the data from Claude's tool_use response.
choices.B: Include detailed JSON formatting instructions and a template example in the system prompt, asking Claude to output only valid JSON.
choices.C: Parse Claude's text response with regex patterns to extract JSON objects, using retry logic for malformed responses.
choices.D: Make two separate API calls—first extracting information as text, then asking Claude to format that text as JSON.

explanation:
A. Define a tool with an input schema matching your required JSON structure and extract the data from
Claude's tool_use response. ✅ (Correct)
Using tool use with a defined schema is the most reliable way to ensure the extracted candidate information
conforms to the required JSON structure. The schema enforces field names, required properties, data types,
and allowed values, significantly reducing validation failures and eliminating the need to parse free-form text.
B. Include detailed JSON formatting instructions and a template example in the system prompt, asking
Claude to output only valid JSON. ❌ (Incorrect)
Prompt instructions improve consistency but cannot guarantee strict schema compliance.
C. Parse Claude's text response with regex patterns to extract JSON objects, using retry logic for
malformed responses. ❌ (Incorrect)
Regex-based parsing is brittle and does not ensure required fields or correct data types.
D. Make two separate API calls—first extracting information as text, then asking Claude to format that
text as JSON. ❌ (Incorrect)
This adds complexity and still does not provide the schema enforcement that tool use offers.\

question: After deploying automated code review, developers report that approximately 35% of flagged findings are false positives falling into consistent patterns: style suggestion contradicting team conventions, security warnings for patterns safe in your deployment context, and performance suggestions that would degrade your specific use ca. You want to reduce false positives while maintaining the ability to catch genuine issues. Which approach best enables the model to generalize its judgment to novel co patterns it hasn't seen before?

choices.A: Include few-shot examples in your prompt showing annotated code snippets that distinguish acceptable patterns from genuine issues in each category.
choices.B: Implement post-processing that uses keyword matching to filter out findings containing terms like "convention," "context-dependent," or "trade-off."
choices.C: Add instructions to your system prompt to "be conservative," "only flag definite issues," and "consider that some patterns may be intentional."
choices.D: Create a comprehensive written specification of all patterns that should not be flagged, then include this full documentation in the system prompt.

explanation:
A. Include few-shot examples in your prompt showing annotated code snippets that distinguish acceptable
patterns from genuine issues in each category. ✅ (Correct)
Few-shot examples teach the model the underlying distinctions between acceptable and problematic
patterns, allowing it to generalize to new, unseen code scenarios. By demonstrating how team conventions,
deployment-specific security considerations, and performance trade-offs should be evaluated, the model
learns the decision-making criteria rather than memorizing specific cases. This is the most effective approach
for reducing systematic false positives while preserving the ability to identify genuine issues.
B. Implement post-processing that uses keyword matching to filter out findings containing terms like
"convention," "context-dependent," or "trade-off." ❌ (Incorrect)
Keyword filtering is brittle and may suppress legitimate findings that happen to contain those terms while
failing to address the root cause of incorrect model reasoning.
C. Add instructions to your system prompt to "be conservative," "only flag definite issues," and "consider
that some patterns may be intentional." ❌ (Incorrect)
General instructions may reduce the number of findings but do not provide enough contextual guidance for
distinguishing acceptable patterns from genuine issues, especially in novel situations.
D. Create a comprehensive written specification of all patterns that should not be flagged, then include this
full documentation in the system prompt. ❌ (Incorrect)
A long list of rules is difficult for the model to apply consistently and may not help it generalize to new
patterns. Examples are typically more effective at teaching nuanced judgment than exhaustive
documentation alone.

question: After expanding the agent's MCP tools with delivery-specific capabilities (check_delivery_status, contact_driver, issue_credit, apply_promo_code, update_delivery_address, reschedule_delivery), the total tool count has grown from 4 to 10. Your evaluation suite shows tool selection accuracy has dropped to 71%. Log analysis reveals the majority of errors involve the agent selecting between semantically overlapping tools-calling issue_credit when process_refund is correct, and calling check_delivery_status when lookup_order already returns the needed data. Which approach structurally eliminates the semantic overlaps that are being logged as the error source?

choices.A: Split the tools across two sub-agents - a "financial resolution" agent with process_refund, issue_credit, and apply_promo_code, and a "delivery" agent with the remaining delivery tools - with a coordinator routing between them.
choices.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct selection for each ambiguous tool pair, such as showing when issue_credit or process_refund is appropriate.
choices.C: Consolidate semantically overlapping tools-merge issue_credit and process_refund into a single resolve_compensation tool with an optional include_tracking flag.
choices.D: Enable the tool search tool with defer_loading on the six new tools, keeping the original four always loaded, so the agent dynamically calls it when needed.

explanation:
C. Consolidate semantically overlapping tools—merge issue_credit and process_refund into a single
resolve_compensation tool with an optional include_tracking flag. ✅ (Correct)
The logged errors stem from semantic overlap between tools, where multiple tools can satisfy similar intents.
Consolidating overlapping tools removes the ambiguity entirely, eliminating the need for the model to choose
between near-equivalent actions. By designing tools around distinct user intents rather than overlapping
implementations, tool selection becomes simpler and more reliable.
A. Split the tools across two sub-agents - a "financial resolution" agent with process_refund, issue_credit,
and apply_promo_code, and a "delivery" agent with the remaining delivery tools - with a coordinator
routing between them. ❌ (Incorrect)
This reduces the number of tools visible to each agent but does not eliminate overlap within a domain. The
financial agent would still need to choose between issue_credit and process_refund.
B. Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct selection for each ambiguous tool
pair, such as showing when issue_credit or process_refund is appropriate. ❌ (Incorrect)
Few-shot examples can improve accuracy but do not remove the underlying ambiguity. The model must still
make a difficult distinction between overlapping tools.
D. Enable the tool search tool with defer_loading on the six new tools, keeping the original four always
loaded, so the agent dynamically calls it when needed. ❌ (Incorrect)
Deferred loading reduces the number of immediately available tools but does not address the root cause of
errors: overlapping tool semantics. Once loaded, the same ambiguity remains.

question: Anthropic's tool use documentation states: "Write instructive error messages. Instead of generic errors like 'failed', include what went wrong and what Claude should try next." A billing dispute agent uses lookup_order, which catches all exceptions and returns a tool_result with is_error: true and the message "execution failed". Monitoring shows two failure modes: the agent retries the identical call until hitting the turn limit, or it immediately calls escalate_to_human without trying alternative tools. Which change follows the documented recommendation and gives Claude the information it needs to select the correct recovery action for each error type?

choices.A: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff inside each tool implementation so transient errors are resolved transparently within the tool before any failure result is surfaced to Claude in the agentic loop.
choices.B: Return error-type-specific messages with is_error: true, e.g., "order not found-try get_customer to search by phone" for data errors and "Database timeout (transient)-retry should succeed" for infrastructure errors.
choices.C: Remove is_error: true and return the error details as normal tool content, so Claude reasons about the response as data rather than treating it as a flagged failure condition that biases retry behavior.
choices.D: Add an error classification step in the agentic loop that intercepts tool errors before Claude sees them, tags each as "retry" "try_alternative," or "escalate," and adds that recommendation to the tool result.

explanation:
B. Return error-type-specific messages with is_error: true, e.g., "order not found—try get_customer to
search by phone" for data errors and "Database timeout (transient)—retry should succeed" for
infrastructure errors. ✅ (Correct)
Anthropic's recommendation is to provide instructive error messages that explain both what went wrong and
what action Claude should take next. Error-specific messages give the model the context needed to
distinguish between failures that warrant a retry and failures that require trying a different tool. This directly
addresses the observed behaviors of repeated identical retries and premature escalation.
A. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff inside each tool implementation so transient errors are
resolved transparently within the tool before any failure result is surfaced to Claude in the agentic loop. ❌
(Incorrect)
Internal retries can help with transient failures, but they do not solve the problem of providing actionable
guidance when an error is ultimately returned to Claude.
C. Remove is_error: true and return the error details as normal tool content, so Claude reasons about the
response as data rather than treating it as a flagged failure condition that biases retry behavior. ❌
(Incorrect)
Errors should still be clearly identified as errors. Removing is_error: true obscures failure states and can lead
to incorrect reasoning about tool outputs.
D. Add an error classification step in the agentic loop that intercepts tool errors before Claude sees them,
tags each as "retry," "try_alternative," or "escalate," and adds that recommendation to the tool result. ❌
(Incorrect)
While potentially helpful, this adds an external classification layer rather than following the documented
guidance. The recommendation is for tools themselves to return informative, actionable error messages that
explain the failure and next step.

question: Your code review prompts include both implementation changes and the corresponding test file, but the LLM's review comments fail to point out untested code paths.Analysis reveals the model correctly flags functions that have no tests at all, but fails to identify when conditional branches or error-handling paths within tested functions that have no tests at all, but fails to identify when conditional branches or error-handling paths within tested functiolack coverage.What's the most effective way to improve detection of branch-level coverage gaps without overcomplicating the pipeline?

choices.A: Implement a multi-pass pipeline where separate LLM calls first extract all conditional branches, then cross- reference each against test assertions in a second pass.
choices.B: Include few-shot examples showing code with an uncovered branch paired with the review comment identifying the specific missing test case.
choices.C: Add explicit instructions directing the model to enumerate each conditional branch and exception path, then verify each has a corresponding test assertion.
choices.D: Restructure the prompt to interleave implementation and tests, presenting each function followed immediately by its test cases

explanation:
B. Include few-shot examples showing code with an uncovered branch paired with the review comment
identifying the specific missing test case. ✅ (Correct)
Few-shot examples are the most effective way to improve the model's ability to recognize branch-level test
coverage gaps and generalize that reasoning to new code. By demonstrating examples where conditional
branches or error-handling paths are untested—and showing the expected review comments—the model
learns the pattern it should look for during review without adding significant pipeline complexity.
A. Implement a multi-pass pipeline where separate LLM calls first extract all conditional branches, then
cross-reference each against test assertions in a second pass. ❌ (Incorrect)
This may improve accuracy but substantially increases system complexity, latency, and cost. The question
asks for improvement without overcomplicating the pipeline.
C. Add explicit instructions directing the model to enumerate each conditional branch and exception path,
then verify each has a corresponding test assertion. ❌ (Incorrect)
Instructions can help, but they are generally less effective than examples at teaching nuanced reasoning
patterns such as identifying branch-level coverage gaps.
D. Restructure the prompt to interleave implementation and tests, presenting each function followed
immediately by its test cases. ❌ (Incorrect)
Improving code-test proximity may aid review, but it does not directly teach the model how to identify missing
coverage for specific branches or error paths.

question: After deploying the automated review, you notice high precision but low recall - real bugs are slipping through undetected. Investigation reveals your review prompt instructs Claude to "only report high-confidence issues you are certain about" and "err on the side of not commenting." Developers appreciate the low noise, but a race condition that caused a production outage was visible in a reviewed PR and went unreported. You need to substantially improve bug detection while keeping false positive rates manageable for your team. What is the most effective approach?

choices.A: Expand the context window by including related test files, recent git history, and the module's dependency graph alongside the diff, giving Claude richer signals to assess issue severity.
choices.B: Remove the conservative filtering instructions and prompt Claude to report all potential issues, then apply a programmatic filter to deduplicate and suppress categories that historically generate false positives.
choices.C: Split the review into a finding stage where Claude's goal is coverage - flagging every potential issue with confidence and severity metadata - and a separate thresholds those findings.
choices.D: Add detailed few-shot examples demonstrating bug categories Claude should flag - race conditions, null dereferences, error handling gaps - while keeping confidence filtering instruction to maintain current precision levels.

explanation:
C. Split the review into a finding stage where Claude's goal is coverage—flagging every potential issue
with confidence and severity metadata—and a separate filtering stage that applies team-specific
thresholds to those findings. ✅ (Correct)
The core problem is that the prompt's conservative filtering instructions are suppressing recall, causing
genuine bugs (such as the race condition) to go unreported. Separating issue detection from issue filtering is
the most effective solution:
Stage 1 (Detection): Maximize recall by identifying all plausible issues and assigning confidence/severity
scores.
Stage 2 (Filtering): Apply team-specific thresholds to control noise and maintain manageable false-positive
rates.
This architecture avoids forcing the model to simultaneously optimize for both recall and precision, which
often leads to missed bugs.
A. Expand the context window by including related test files, recent git history, and the module's
dependency graph alongside the diff, giving Claude richer signals to assess issue severity. ❌ (Incorrect)
More context can help in some cases, but it does not address the primary issue: the prompt explicitly tells
Claude to suppress anything it is not certain about.
B. Remove the conservative filtering instructions and prompt Claude to report all potential issues, then
apply a programmatic filter to deduplicate and suppress categories that historically generate false
positives. ❌ (Incorrect)
This improves recall but relies on coarse category-level filtering rather than evaluating each finding's
confidence and severity. It is less flexible and effective than a dedicated detection-and-filtering pipeline.
D. Add detailed few-shot examples demonstrating bug categories Claude should flag—race conditions,
null dereferences, error handling gaps—while keeping confidence filtering instructions to maintain current
precision levels. ❌ (Incorrect)
Examples may help identify more bug patterns, but the conservative instructions would still suppress many
findings, leaving the recall problem largely unresolved.

question: Your automated reviewer uses a single prompt covering security issues, API design, and business logic correctness. Your evaluation suite shows strong recall findings (82%) but poor recall for business logic edge cases in quiz scoring (34%). When you add few-shot examples of logic bugs to the prompt, logic recall is 41% but API design recall drops to 68%. How should you address this trade-off to improve detection across both categories?

choices.A: Split the review into separate focused prompts - one for security and API design, another for business logic - each with dedicated examples, then combine findings before posting.
choices.B: Upgrade to a more capable model tier, since its stronger reasoning will handle both concern types in a single prompt and eliminate the recall trade-off.
choices.C: Provide the full repository as context instead of just the changed files and surrounding code, giving the model deeper visibility into business logic.
choices.D: Replace the few-shot examples with a detailed checklist of specific logic edge cases to verify, such as division-by-zero in score calculation or grading thresholds.

explanation:
A. Split the review into separate focused prompts - one for security and API design, another for business
logic - each with dedicated examples, then combine findings before posting. ✅ (Correct)
The results show a classic prompt-capacity trade-off: adding business-logic examples improves logic recall
but reduces API design recall. By separating concerns into specialized review passes, each prompt can focus
on its domain with targeted instructions and examples without competing for attention or context. The
findings can then be merged into a single review output, improving overall recall across categories.
B. Upgrade to a more capable model tier, since its stronger reasoning will handle both concern types in a
single prompt and eliminate the recall trade-off. ❌ (Incorrect)
A stronger model may help, but it does not fundamentally solve the prompt specialization problem. The trade-
off can still persist when multiple review objectives compete within one prompt.
C. Provide the full repository as context instead of just the changed files and surrounding code, giving the
model deeper visibility into business logic. ❌ (Incorrect)
More context increases cost and complexity and may even dilute attention. The observed issue stems from
balancing multiple review goals, not a lack of repository-wide context.
D. Replace the few-shot examples with a detailed checklist of specific logic edge cases to verify, such as
division-by-zero in score calculation or grading thresholds. ❌ (Incorrect)
Checklists can help for known cases but are less effective than dedicated, specialized review passes. They
also do not address the underlying competition between business-logic and API-design review objectives
within a single prompt.

question: Your agent is handling a billing dispute. After calling get_customer and lookup_order, it identifies that the dispute involves a promotional pricing eroapproval—beyond the agent's authorization level. How should the workflow handle this mid-process escalation?

choices.A: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call escalate_to_human with a reference ID.
choices.B: Compile a structured handoff with customer details, order info, and the identified issue before calling escalate_to_human
choices.C: Attempt the refund with process refund anyway, escalating only if the system rejects the transaction.
choices.D: Call escalate_to_human passing only the customer's original message.

explanation:
B. Compile a structured handoff with customer details, order info, and the identified issue before calling
escalate_to_human. ✅ (Correct)
When an agent determines that a case requires human approval or exceeds its authorization level, it should
create a structured handoff containing all relevant context gathered during the investigation. This enables
the human agent to continue from the current state without repeating work and ensures a smooth transition.
A. Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call escalate_to_human
with a reference ID. ❌ (Incorrect)
While storing history may be useful, providing only a reference ID does not ensure the human agent receives a
clear summary of the issue or the investigation already performed.
C. Attempt the refund with process_refund anyway, escalating only if the system rejects the transaction.
❌ (Incorrect)
The scenario states that the refund amount exceeds the agent's authorization level. The agent should respect
authorization boundaries and escalate rather than attempting the action.
D. Call escalate_to_human passing only the customer's original message. ❌ (Incorrect)
Passing only the original message omits the customer information, order details, and findings already
collected, forcing the human agent to repeat the investigation.

question: Your agent has called lookup_order multiple times while investigating a customer's return requests. Each response includes 40+ fields (items, shipping details, payment info, status history). Tool outputs now represent the majority of the conversation's context. The customer mentions two more orders they want to discuss. What's the effective approach before making additional lookups?97

choices.A: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing order response, removing verbose details
choices.B: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output context
choices.C: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing, retrieving relevant portions as the conversation continues
choices.D: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order's key details, replacing structured responses with prose descriptions

explanation:
A. Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing
order response, removing verbose details. ✅ (Correct)
As tool outputs begin to dominate the context window, the best approach is to compress and retain only the
information relevant to the current task. Since the conversation is focused on return requests, fields such as
items, purchase date, return eligibility, and order status should be preserved, while unrelated details like
payment information, full shipping records, and extensive status history can be removed. This maintains the
necessary context while conserving tokens for future interactions and tool calls.
B. Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output context. ❌ (Incorrect)
Continuing to accumulate large tool responses wastes context window space and increases the risk of losing
important information later in the conversation.
C. Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing, retrieving relevant portions as the
conversation continues. ❌ (Incorrect)
While retrieval systems can be useful for long-term memory, this is a more complex solution than necessary.
The immediate issue is excessive tool output, which can be addressed more effectively through targeted
context reduction.
D. Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order's key details, replacing structured
responses with prose descriptions. ❌ (Incorrect)
Natural language summaries may omit important fields and are generally less reliable than retaining a
compact, structured representation of the relevant data.

question: Production logs show that when the agent handles complex billing disputes requiring 6+ tool calls, it sometimes exhausts its max_turns limit after gathering data and before completing resolution or escalating. The team's goal is to guarantee that every customer interaction ends with either a completed resolution or a human escalation, regardless of how the agent loop terminates. Which approach achieves this guarantee?

choices.A: Add orchestration-layer code that checks the agent's outcome after each loop termination - if the loop ended without a completed resolution or escalation, programmatically call escalate_to_human with the accumulated conversation context and tool results.
choices.B: Implement a pre-tool-use hook that counts tool invocations and terminates the loop with an automatic escalation once the agent reaches 80% of its remaining actions.
choices.C: Add system prompt instructions telling the agent to call escalate_to_human with a summary of its findings whenever it determines it cannot resolve the dispute.
choices.D: Split the workflow into two sequential agent invocations — a first agent gathers information via get_customer and lookup_order, then the second agent uses that data and handles process_refund or escalate_to_human, each with separate turn budgets.

explanation:
A. Add orchestration-layer code that checks the agent's outcome after each loop termination - if the loop
ended without a completed resolution or escalation, programmatically call escalate_to_human with the
accumulated conversation context and tool results. ✅ (Correct)
This is the only option that guarantees every interaction ends in either a successful resolution or a human
escalation. By enforcing the check outside the agent loop, the system remains robust even if the agent
exhausts its max_turns, encounters unexpected behavior, or fails to explicitly escalate. The orchestration
layer acts as a reliable fallback that ensures no case is left unresolved.
B. Implement a pre-tool-use hook that counts tool invocations and terminates the loop with an automatic
escalation once the agent reaches 80% of its remaining actions. ❌ (Incorrect)
This may reduce the likelihood of hitting the turn limit, but it does not guarantee resolution or escalation in all
termination scenarios.
C. Add system prompt instructions telling the agent to call escalate_to_human with a summary of its
findings whenever it determines it cannot resolve the dispute. ❌ (Incorrect)
Prompt instructions improve behavior but cannot provide a hard guarantee, especially when the agent
reaches max_turns before making that decision.
D. Split the workflow into two sequential agent invocations — a first agent gathers information via
get_customer and lookup_order, then the second agent uses that data and handles process_refund or
escalate_to_human, each with separate turn budgets. ❌ (Incorrect)
This may reduce turn-limit issues but still does not guarantee that one of the agents will successfully resolve
or escalate every case. An external enforcement mechanism is still required.

question: The document analysis agent has a single analyze_document tool that takes a document and a free-text instruction parameter. During evaluation, requests like "key financial metrics" often return narrative summaries, while "summarize the methodology" sometimes returns raw data tables. The synthesis agent reports analysis results require re-requests with clarified instructions. What's the most effective way to improve reliability?

choices.A: Enhance the tool description with detailed examples showing how different instruction phrasings should map to different output formats
choices.B: Keep the single tool but add an analysis type enum parameter requiring explicit selection between extraction, summarization, and verification
choices.C: Have the coordinator pre-classify each analysis request before passing instructions to the document analysis agent
choices.D: Split the generic tool into purpose specific tools—extract data points, summarize content, verify claim against source—each with

explanation:
D. Split the generic tool into purpose-specific tools—extract data points, summarize content, verify claims
against source—each with clearly defined inputs and outputs. ✅ (Correct)
The root cause is that a single tool with a free-text instruction parameter has ambiguous semantics, leading
to inconsistent outputs. Splitting it into purpose-specific tools removes this ambiguity by giving each tool a
clear responsibility and expected output format. This makes tool selection more reliable and reduces the need
for re-requests or clarification by downstream agents.
A. Enhance the tool description with detailed examples showing how different instruction phrasings should
map to different output formats. ❌ (Incorrect)
Examples may improve performance, but the tool still relies on interpreting free-text instructions, leaving
ambiguity and inconsistency in place.
B. Keep the single tool but add an analysis type enum parameter requiring explicit selection between
extraction, summarization, and verification. ❌ (Incorrect)
An enum helps reduce ambiguity, but the tool still combines multiple responsibilities. Purpose-specific tools
generally provide clearer semantics and more reliable behavior.
C. Have the coordinator pre-classify each analysis request before passing instructions to the document
analysis agent. ❌ (Incorrect)
This shifts the classification burden elsewhere but does not solve the underlying issue that the tool itself
performs multiple distinct tasks with overlapping behavior.

question: In production, you observe that simple fact-checking queries (e.g., "What year was the Paris Climate Agreement signed?") traverse all four subagents sequentially, consuming 40+ seconds and significant tokens per query. Complex comparative research benefits from the full pipeline. Your query distribution is diverse and evolving as users discover new applications. What's the most effective approach to optimize for varying query complexity?

choices.A: Have the coordinator analyze each query and dynamically decide which subagents to invoke based on its assessment of query requirements.
choices.B: Train a query complexity classifier on labeled historical data to predict optimal subagent combinations, retraining periodically as query patterns evolve.
choices.C: Create a fast-path for factual questions that bypasses subagents entirely, routing all other queries through the complete pipeline to ensure research thorough
choices.D: Implement pattern-based routing that categorizes queries by structure (single-fact vs. comparative vs. analytical) and maps each category to a predefined combination.

explanation:
A. Have the coordinator analyze each query and dynamically decide which subagents to invoke based on its
assessment of query requirements. ❌ (Incorrect)
This relies on runtime LLM judgment for every request, which adds latency and inconsistency. It is less
efficient than a learned routing policy and does not scale well as query patterns diversify.
B. Train a query complexity classifier on labeled historical data to predict optimal subagent combinations,
retraining periodically as query patterns evolve. ✅ (Correct)
A trained classifier provides fast, consistent routing decisions based on learned patterns from historical data.
It reduces latency by avoiding full LLM-based orchestration per query and adapts over time through retraining
as usage patterns shift.
C. Create a fast-path for factual questions that bypasses subagents entirely, routing all other queries
through the complete pipeline to ensure research thoroughness. ❌ (Incorrect)
This improves performance for a subset of queries but creates a rigid binary system that cannot handle
intermediate or evolving query complexities effectively.
D. Implement pattern-based routing that categorizes queries by structure (single-fact vs. comparative vs.
analytical) and maps each category to a predefined combination. ❌ (Incorrect)
Rule-based pattern routing is brittle and struggles to generalize to new or ambiguous query types, requiring
frequent manual updates as usage evolves.

question: Your multi-agent research pipeline crashed after processing 12 of 28 documents. The web search agent had identified relevant sources, the document analyzer had partially completed extraction, and the synthesizer had begun pattern identification. You need to resume processing without repeating work or losing fidelity of prior findings. What state management approach best balances information fidelity with context efficiency when restoring agent state?

choices.A: Index all agent outputs in a shared vector store. When resuming, each agent queries the store using semantic search to retrieve relevant prior findings.
choices.B: Have each agent maintain its own persistent state file and reload it independently at the start of each session.
choices.C: Have each agent persist a structured export to a known location. On resume, the coordinator loads the manifest and injects relevant state into agent prompts.
choices.D: Persist the coordinator's conversation log containing all task delegations and responses, providing this to agents when resuming.

explanation:
C. Have each agent persist a structured export to a known location. On resume, the coordinator loads the
manifest and injects relevant state into agent prompts. ✅ (Correct)
This approach preserves structured, deterministic intermediate state from each agent, which is critical for
resuming a partially completed multi-agent pipeline without redoing work. The coordinator can reconstruct
the workflow precisely by loading a manifest of completed and partial results, then selectively passing only
the required context back into each agent.
It avoids redundant computation, prevents loss of fidelity, and keeps context usage efficient by not
reintroducing full logs or relying on probabilistic retrieval mechanisms.
A. Index all agent outputs in a shared vector store. When resuming, each agent queries the store using
semantic search to retrieve relevant prior findings. ❌ (Incorrect)
Vector search introduces non-deterministic retrieval, which can miss exact intermediate structured outputs
needed for correct continuation of a pipeline.
B. Have each agent maintain its own persistent state file and reload it independently at the start of each
session. ❌ (Incorrect)
This leads to fragmented state ownership, making cross-agent coordination harder and increasing the risk of
inconsistencies in shared workflow context.
D. Persist the coordinator's conversation log containing all task delegations and responses, providing this
to agents when resuming. ❌ (Incorrect)
Raw logs are unstructured and inefficient, and may include irrelevant context that wastes tokens and
reduces clarity when resuming execution.

question: Your agent has analyzed a complex service module—reading 23 source files, tracing request flows, and identifying error handling patterns. A developer needs to develop two testing strategies before committing to one: end-to-end tests with mocked external services vs. snapshot tests capturing expected outputs. The developer needs to independently develop both approaches to evaluate trade-offs. How should you manage the sessions?

choices.A: Resume the analysis session with fork_session enabled, creating a separate branch for each testing strategy.
choices.B: Export the analysis session's key findings to a file, then create two new sessions that reference this file.
choices.C: Continue in the original session, developing end-to-end tests first, then snapshot tests sequentially.
choices.D: Start two fresh sessions, having each re-read the relevant source files before beginning.

explanation:
A. Resume the analysis session with fork_session enabled, creating a separate branch for each testing
strategy. ✅ (Correct)
Forking the session is the most effective way to support parallel, independent exploration while preserving
the full analysis context in-memory. Since the agent has already completed a heavy analysis phase (23
source files, request flows, error handling), that state should not be recomputed or exported—it should be
reused directly.
With fork_session, each branch inherits the same authoritative context and can independently explore end-to-
end tests vs snapshot tests without interference, ordering bias, or redundant reprocessing. This makes it ideal
for evaluating trade-offs under identical starting conditions.
B. Export the analysis session's key findings to a file, then create two new sessions that reference this file.
❌ (Incorrect)
This introduces unnecessary serialization and reconstruction overhead. It also risks losing subtle contextual
relationships present in the live session state.
C. Continue in the original session, developing end-to-end tests first, then snapshot tests sequentially. ❌
(Incorrect)
Sequential execution prevents independent evaluation and introduces bias from the order of exploration.
D. Start two fresh sessions, having each re-read the relevant source files before beginning. ❌ (Incorrect)
This wastes computation by repeating the expensive 23-file analysis instead of reusing already established
context.

question: Production monitoring shows the research phase takes longer than expected. Analysis reveals the coordinator invokes the web search subagent, then invokes the document analysis subagent and waits again. These tasks are independent—neither requires the other's output. What is the most effective way to run these subagents concurrently?

choices.A: Switch both subagents to use a Haiku-tier model instead of Sonnet to reduce their individual execution time.
choices.B: Create an async orchestration layer outside the agent that spawns parallel threads, each running a separate coordinator.
choices.C: Structure the coordinator to emit both Task tool calls (for web search and document analysis) in a single response message.
choices.D: Add detailed instructions to the coordinator's system prompt explaining the performance benefits of parallel execution at the same time.

explanation:
C. Structure the coordinator to emit both Task tool calls (for web search and document analysis) in a single
response message. ✅ (Correct)
This enables true concurrent execution at the tool-calling layer. Since the web search and document
analysis subagents are independent, issuing both tool calls in the same coordinator response allows the
runtime to execute them in parallel instead of waiting for one to finish before starting the other. This directly
removes the sequential bottleneck without changing model quality or adding unnecessary system complexity.
A. Switch both subagents to use a Haiku-tier model instead of Sonnet to reduce their individual execution
time. ❌ (Incorrect)
This reduces per-task latency but does not address the core inefficiency, which is sequential execution, not
model speed.
B. Create an async orchestration layer outside the agent that spawns parallel threads, each running a
separate coordinator. ❌ (Incorrect)
This introduces unnecessary architectural complexity and duplicates coordination logic when parallelism can
already be achieved within a single coordinator response.
D. Add detailed instructions to the coordinator's system prompt explaining the performance benefits of
parallel execution at the same time. ❌ (Incorrect)
Prompt instructions alone cannot guarantee parallel execution; only structured multi-tool invocation can
enforce concurrency.

question: An engineer used the agent yesterday to analyze a legacy authentication module, identifying two distinct refactoring approaches: extracting a microservice versus refactoring in-place. Today, they want to explore both approaches in depth—having the agent propose specific code changes for each—before deciding which to implement. What's the most effective way to structure this exploration?

choices.A: Use fork_session to create two branches from yesterday's analysis, exploring one approach in each fork.
choices.B: Resume yesterday's session and explore both approaches sequentially within the same conversation thread.
choices.C: Resume yesterday's session to explore the first approach, then start a new session for the second, manually recreating the original context.
choices.D: Start two fresh sessions, manually providing a summary of yesterday's analysis findings to establish context.

explanation:
A. Use fork_session to create two branches from yesterday's analysis, exploring one approach in each fork.
❌ (Incorrect)
Forking is best when you need parallel exploration of competing directions without shared context. In this
case, both refactoring approaches are being evaluated from the same analytical baseline and are meant to be
compared within a single continuous reasoning flow. Forking adds unnecessary fragmentation.
B. Resume yesterday's session and explore both approaches sequentially within the same conversation
thread. ✅ (Correct)
This preserves full historical context from the original analysis while allowing structured, step-by-step
comparison of both refactoring strategies. Since both approaches depend on the same prior findings, keeping
them in one session ensures consistency, reduces duplication, and makes trade-off evaluation easier.
C. Resume yesterday's session to explore the first approach, then start a new session for the second,
manually recreating the original context. ❌ (Incorrect)
This introduces avoidable overhead and risks inconsistencies when manually reconstructing context. It also
breaks continuity, making it harder to directly compare the two approaches under identical assumptions.
D. Start two fresh sessions, manually providing a summary of yesterday's analysis findings to establish
context. ❌ (Incorrect)
This is the least efficient option. It relies entirely on manual summarization, which can omit important details
and lead to context drift. It also duplicates effort and reduces reliability compared to continuing the original
session.

question: An engineer asks your agent to add comprehensive tests to a legacy codebase with 200 files and minimal existing test coverage. The engineer hasn't spent much time on the codebase, so they ask for help identifying which modules to prioritize. How should the agent decompose this open-ended task?

choices.A: Use Glob and Grep to map codebase structure, identify heavily-coupled modules, create a prioritized plan for high-impact areas, and revise as dependencies are discovered.
choices.B: Create a fixed testing schedule upfront based on directory structure, allocating equal effort to each top- level directory regardless of code complexity or importance.
choices.C: Systematically read all 200 files to create a complete function inventory before writing any tests, ensuring the testing plan accounts for everything from the beginning.
choices.D: Start writing tests for the first module alphabetically, using test failures and imports to discover related files organically.

explanation:
A. Use Glob and Grep to map codebase structure, identify heavily-coupled modules, create a prioritized
plan for high-impact areas, and revise as dependencies are discovered. ✅ (Correct)
This is the most effective decomposition strategy because it uses lightweight exploration tools first to
understand structure and coupling, then prioritizes testing based on impact and dependency density. It is
iterative and adapts as new relationships between modules are discovered, which is essential in large, poorly
tested codebases.
B. Create a fixed testing schedule upfront based on directory structure, allocating equal effort to each top-
level directory regardless of code complexity or importance. ❌ (Incorrect)
This ignores differences in module complexity, risk, and coupling. Treating all directories equally leads to
inefficient allocation of testing effort and misses high-risk areas that need deeper coverage.
C. Systematically read all 200 files to create a complete function inventory before writing any tests,
ensuring the testing plan accounts for everything from the beginning. ❌ (Incorrect)
While thorough, this is not scalable and delays actual testing work. It front-loads too much analysis and
defeats the purpose of incremental discovery, especially in large legacy systems.
D. Start writing tests for the first module alphabetically, using test failures and imports to discover related

question: An agent SDK harness runs a customer-support triage loop. On iteration 4, the API response contains no tool_use content block, only the text "I've created ticket #8842 and notified the warehouse team." The stop_reason field on that same response reads "end_turn." A reviewer proposes that the harness should terminate the loop by scanning the assistant's text for phrases like "created" or "notified" to detect completion. What is the correct way for the harness to decide whether to stop iterating?

choices.A: Scan the assistant's text output for completion-sounding phrases such as "created" or "done," and stop once one is found.
choices.B: Inspect the response's stop_reason field programmatically: continue feeding tool results back into the loop when it equals "tool_use," and stop the loop only when it equals "end_turn."
choices.C: Terminate the loop once a fixed number of iterations (e.g., 4) has been reached, independent of the response content.
choices.D: Stop as soon as the assistant's response contains any punctuation-terminated sentence, since that indicates the model believes it is finished.

explanation:
A. Scan the assistant's text output for completion-sounding phrases such as "created" or "done," and stop once one is found. ❌ Incorrect.
Parsing natural-language text for semantic cues is brittle — phrasing varies, and the model can describe an action without having actually finished the loop, or vice versa, leading to premature or missed termination.
B. Inspect the response's stop_reason field programmatically: continue feeding tool results back into the loop when it equals "tool_use," and stop the loop only when it equals "end_turn." ✅ Correct.
stop_reason is a structured, deterministic API field designed exactly for this purpose: "tool_use" means the model is waiting on tool results and the loop must continue, while "end_turn" means the model has produced its final response and the loop should terminate.
C. Terminate the loop once a fixed number of iterations (e.g., 4) has been reached, independent of the response content. ❌ Incorrect.
An arbitrary iteration cap ignores what the model actually signaled and will cut off legitimately longer tasks or fail to stop early when the model is genuinely done, making it an unreliable primary termination mechanism.
D. Stop as soon as the assistant's response contains any punctuation-terminated sentence, since that indicates the model believes it is finished. ❌ Incorrect.
Sentence structure has no relationship to task completion; this is an even less reliable heuristic than keyword matching and would terminate loops mid-task constantly.

question: A document-processing agent was given a safety limit of max_iterations = 8 to prevent runaway loops. In production, a batch of unusually large contracts requires 11 rounds of tool calls to fully extract and validate all clauses. The harness halts the agent at iteration 8, returning whatever partial output exists in the last assistant message, with no error raised and no indication to the caller that the task was cut short. What is the primary architectural flaw here, and how should it be fixed?

choices.A: The flaw is that 8 iterations is too low a number; simply raising the constant to a higher fixed value like 20 fully resolves the issue.
choices.B: The flaw is that the harness is using the iteration cap as the primary stop-condition instead of stop_reason; the cap should exist only as a high-ceiling safety net for runaway loops, while stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn") should drive normal termination, with truncation-by-cap explicitly surfaced as an error/warning rather than silently returned as a result.
choices.C: The flaw is that the agent should never call tools more than a handful of times, so the contract-extraction workflow itself needs to be redesigned to avoid tool calls entirely.
choices.D: The flaw is unrelated to iteration limits; the real issue is that the model's context window is too small to hold 11 rounds of conversation.

explanation:
A. The flaw is that 8 iterations is too low a number; simply raising the constant to a higher fixed value like 20 fully resolves the issue. ❌ Incorrect.
Raising the number only delays the same failure mode to a larger contract; it doesn't fix the underlying problem of treating an arbitrary cap as the authoritative stop signal instead of stop_reason.
B. The flaw is that the harness is using the iteration cap as the primary stop-condition instead of stop_reason; the cap should exist only as a high-ceiling safety net for runaway loops, while stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn") should drive normal termination, with truncation-by-cap explicitly surfaced as an error/warning rather than silently returned as a result. ✅ Correct.
The loop should keep running as long as stop_reason is "tool_use" and stop when it's "end_turn"; an iteration cap is a legitimate defensive mechanism against genuinely runaway behavior, but it must be set generously and, when triggered, must surface as an explicit failure state rather than being confused with normal completion.
C. The flaw is that the agent should never call tools more than a handful of times, so the contract-extraction workflow itself needs to be redesigned to avoid tool calls entirely. ❌ Incorrect.
Many legitimate tasks genuinely require many sequential tool calls; the fix is correct control-flow logic, not eliminating tool use.
D. The flaw is unrelated to iteration limits; the real issue is that the model's context window is too small to hold 11 rounds of conversation. ❌ Incorrect.
The symptom described (silent truncation at exactly iteration 8) is a direct consequence of the hardcoded cap, not a context-window overflow, and nothing in the scenario indicates a context-length error occurred.

question: A coordinator agent is configured to delegate specialized subtasks to a "data-validator" subagent using the Task tool. In production, every attempt to invoke the data-validator subagent fails immediately with a permissions error, even though the AgentDefinition for "data-validator" is correctly registered and its own system prompt and tool list look fine. What is the most likely root cause?

choices.A: The data-validator AgentDefinition's system prompt is too short to give the model enough guidance to act correctly.
choices.B: The coordinator's own allowedTools list does not include "Task", so the coordinator itself is not permitted to invoke the spawn mechanism regardless of how the target subagent is configured.
choices.C: The data-validator subagent needs its own copy of the entire conversation history in order to be invoked at all.
choices.D: The Task tool can only be used to spawn subagents that have identical tool permissions to the coordinator.

explanation:
A. The data-validator AgentDefinition's system prompt is too short to give the model enough guidance to act correctly. ❌ Incorrect.
A short system prompt might reduce output quality but would not cause an immediate permissions error when attempting to invoke the subagent at all.
B. The coordinator's own allowedTools list does not include "Task", so the coordinator itself is not permitted to invoke the spawn mechanism regardless of how the target subagent is configured. ✅ Correct.
The Task tool is the mechanism by which a coordinator spawns subagents; if "Task" is missing from the coordinator's own allowedTools, every delegation attempt is blocked at the coordinator level, independent of how well-formed the target subagent's AgentDefinition is.
C. The data-validator subagent needs its own copy of the entire conversation history in order to be invoked at all. ❌ Incorrect.
Subagents run in isolated context and are invoked via an explicit prompt; lack of inherited history does not by itself produce a permissions error, it produces a subagent that lacks context.
D. The Task tool can only be used to spawn subagents that have identical tool permissions to the coordinator. ❌ Incorrect.
Subagents are commonly configured with narrower, task-specific tool restrictions than the coordinator; identical permissions are not a requirement of the Task tool.

question: A coordinator agent processes a multi-turn conversation in which the user specifies that all invoice amounts should be converted to EUR before validation. Later, the coordinator spawns an "invoice-checker" subagent via the Task tool with the prompt "Check invoice #4521 for errors." The subagent replies asking which currency the invoice amounts should be interpreted in, since the raw invoice is in USD. Why did this happen?

choices.A: The invoice-checker subagent has a bug in its AgentDefinition that prevents it from reading currency fields.
choices.B: The Task tool automatically strips currency-related information from any prompt it forwards to a subagent.
choices.C: Subagents run with isolated context and do not automatically inherit the coordinator's prior conversation history; because the EUR-conversion requirement was never included in the prompt passed to the subagent, the subagent had no way to know about it.
choices.D: The subagent exceeded its max_iterations before it could infer the currency requirement from context.

explanation:
A. The invoice-checker subagent has a bug in its AgentDefinition that prevents it from reading currency fields. ❌ Incorrect.
Nothing in the scenario suggests a parsing defect; the subagent is asking a clarifying question precisely because it was never told the requirement, not because it failed to read a field it had access to.
B. The Task tool automatically strips currency-related information from any prompt it forwards to a subagent. ❌ Incorrect.
The Task tool does not selectively filter content out of prompts; it forwards whatever prompt text the coordinator constructs, so the coordinator itself never included the requirement.
C. Subagents run with isolated context and do not automatically inherit the coordinator's prior conversation history; because the EUR-conversion requirement was never included in the prompt passed to the subagent, the subagent had no way to know about it. ✅ Correct.
Hub-and-spoke subagents start with a clean, isolated context window per invocation; the coordinator is responsible for explicitly restating any relevant prior context (like the EUR conversion instruction) inside the Task prompt, since nothing is auto-inherited.
D. The subagent exceeded its max_iterations before it could infer the currency requirement from context. ❌ Incorrect.
The subagent asked a direct clarifying question rather than being truncated mid-task, which is inconsistent with an iteration-limit failure.

question: A team defines a "code-reviewer" subagent whose AgentDefinition grants it Read, Grep, Bash, and Write tools, intending for it to only read and comment on code. During a routine pull-request review, the subagent unexpectedly runs a Write call that reformats one of the files it was reviewing, which then gets flagged in the PR diff as an unrelated change. What is the most effective fix?

choices.A: Add a sentence to the coordinator's prompt instructing it to remind the code-reviewer subagent not to modify files.
choices.B: Increase the code-reviewer subagent's max_iterations so it has more room to reconsider before writing.
choices.C: Restrict the code-reviewer AgentDefinition's allowedTools to only Read and Grep, removing Bash and Write so that file modification is structurally impossible for that subagent type, regardless of what the model decides to do.
choices.D: Switch the code-reviewer subagent to use fork_session so that any file writes happen on an isolated branch instead of the main session.

explanation:
A. Add a sentence to the coordinator's prompt instructing it to remind the code-reviewer subagent not to modify files. ❌ Incorrect.
Prompt-based instructions to avoid an available capability have a non-zero failure rate; the model can still choose to call Write since the tool remains accessible.
B. Increase the code-reviewer subagent's max_iterations so it has more room to reconsider before writing. ❌ Incorrect.
More iterations does not address the root cause, which is that a read-only subagent has write access it should never have needed in the first place.
C. Restrict the code-reviewer AgentDefinition's allowedTools to only Read and Grep, removing Bash and Write so that file modification is structurally impossible for that subagent type, regardless of what the model decides to do. ✅ Correct.
AgentDefinition-level tool restrictions provide a deterministic guarantee: if Write and Bash are not in the subagent's allowedTools, the model has no capability to modify files no matter what it reasons its way into, which is far more reliable than a prompt-based reminder.
D. Switch the code-reviewer subagent to use fork_session so that any file writes happen on an isolated branch instead of the main session. ❌ Incorrect.
fork_session is a mechanism for branching context to explore divergent approaches; it does not restrict tool access and would not prevent the subagent from writing files.

question: A pricing team has already spent significant agent time building a 50-page market-analysis baseline session covering competitor pricing and customer price sensitivity. From that same baseline, they now want to independently explore two different promotional strategies — a tiered-volume-discount approach and a bundle-discount approach — without either exploration's follow-up questions or draft numbers contaminating the other, and without redoing the market analysis. What is the most effective way to structure this?

choices.A: Continue in the single existing session, exploring the tiered-discount strategy first and then the bundle-discount strategy afterward, in the same context.
choices.B: Use fork_session twice from the completed market-analysis session to create two independent branches, one for exploring the tiered-discount strategy and one for the bundle-discount strategy, each starting from the same shared baseline context.
choices.C: Start two entirely new sessions from scratch and manually re-run the market analysis in each one before beginning the pricing exploration.
choices.D: Spawn a single subagent via the Task tool and instruct it to alternate between the two strategies within one conversation turn.

explanation:
A. Continue in the single existing session, exploring the tiered-discount strategy first and then the bundle-discount strategy afterward, in the same context. ❌ Incorrect.
Working both strategies sequentially in one shared session means each exploration's reasoning, assumptions, and draft numbers accumulate in the same context and can bleed into or bias the other, defeating the goal of independent exploration.
B. Use fork_session twice from the completed market-analysis session to create two independent branches, one for exploring the tiered-discount strategy and one for the bundle-discount strategy, each starting from the same shared baseline context. ✅ Correct.
fork_session is designed exactly for this case: branching from a shared analysis baseline into independent sessions lets each divergent strategy be explored in isolation, with neither branch polluting the other, while avoiding the cost of redoing the market analysis.
C. Start two entirely new sessions from scratch and manually re-run the market analysis in each one before beginning the pricing exploration. ❌ Incorrect.
This discards the already-completed baseline work and duplicates effort unnecessarily; forking preserves the baseline without re-running it.
D. Spawn a single subagent via the Task tool and instruct it to alternate between the two strategies within one conversation turn. ❌ Incorrect.
Alternating within one subagent invocation still shares a single context, so the two strategies are not actually isolated from each other.

question: An engineer resumes a named session, "db-migration-audit," three days after it was last active, using --resume db-migration-audit. The session's cached tool results include a schema-inspection query run before a migration was deployed to production over the weekend; the underlying table structure has since changed. If the engineer simply resumes and continues asking questions, the agent will reason over the stale cached schema as if it were still current. What is the most effective way to proceed?

choices.A: Resume the named session as usual and trust the model to notice on its own that three days have passed and re-verify the schema before answering.
choices.B: Avoid resuming altogether; start a completely fresh session with no reference to the prior investigation, since the schema has changed.
choices.C: Resume the named session, but before proceeding, either explicitly inform the agent that the schema changed since the migration and re-run the relevant tool calls, or, if most of the session's cached results are now stale, start a fresh session and inject a structured summary of the still-valid prior findings instead of relying on outdated cached tool output.
choices.D: Rename the session so the agent treats it as a brand-new investigation while keeping all the old cached tool results intact.

explanation:
A. Resume the named session as usual and trust the model to notice on its own that three days have passed and re-verify the schema before answering. ❌ Incorrect.
The model has no inherent awareness of real-world elapsed time or out-of-band changes like a weekend migration; it will treat the cached tool results as current unless told otherwise.
B. Avoid resuming altogether; start a completely fresh session with no reference to the prior investigation, since the schema has changed. ❌ Incorrect.
Discarding the prior investigation entirely wastes the valid parts of the earlier analysis that are unaffected by the schema change; a full restart with no context is more wasteful than necessary.
C. Resume the named session, but before proceeding, either explicitly inform the agent that the schema changed since the migration and re-run the relevant tool calls, or, if most of the session's cached results are now stale, start a fresh session and inject a structured summary of the still-valid prior findings instead of relying on outdated cached tool output. ✅ Correct.
When prior tool results may be stale, the choice is between resuming with an explicit correction (re-running affected tool calls) or starting fresh with a structured summary of what's still valid — either way, stale tool output must not be silently trusted as current state.
D. Rename the session so the agent treats it as a brand-new investigation while keeping all the old cached tool results intact. ❌ Incorrect.
Renaming has no effect on whether cached tool results are stale; the underlying problem of outdated schema information in the cache remains untouched.

question: A coordinator needs to run two independent audits, an "seo-audit" subagent and a "performance-audit" subagent, neither of which depends on the other's output. Currently, the coordinator's response invokes the seo-audit Task call, waits for it to finish (18 seconds), and only in a subsequent turn invokes the performance-audit Task call, waiting another 18 seconds, for a total of 36 seconds. What change would most effectively reduce this latency while preserving correctness?

choices.A: Merge both audits into a single subagent AgentDefinition so only one Task call is ever needed.
choices.B: Increase the max_iterations for the seo-audit subagent so it finishes faster.
choices.C: Emit both the seo-audit and performance-audit Task tool calls within the same coordinator response, so the SDK dispatches them concurrently instead of the coordinator waiting for one to complete before issuing the other in a later turn.
choices.D: Have the coordinator fork_session before invoking seo-audit so the performance-audit can run in the forked branch simultaneously.

explanation:
A. Merge both audits into a single subagent AgentDefinition so only one Task call is ever needed. ❌ Incorrect.
Collapsing two independent, differently-specialized responsibilities into one subagent sacrifices the benefits of specialization and isolation and doesn't fundamentally address the sequencing problem; it just hides it inside one subagent's internal logic.
B. Increase the max_iterations for the seo-audit subagent so it finishes faster. ❌ Incorrect.
Raising an iteration cap does not affect wall-clock latency of a subagent that is already completing successfully in 18 seconds; the bottleneck is the sequential waiting pattern, not iteration limits.
C. Emit both the seo-audit and performance-audit Task tool calls within the same coordinator response, so the SDK dispatches them concurrently instead of the coordinator waiting for one to complete before issuing the other in a later turn. ✅ Correct.
When multiple Task tool calls for independent subagents are emitted together in a single coordinator response, the SDK can execute them in parallel; issuing them across separate turns forces sequential waiting, roughly doubling latency as seen in this 36-second example.
D. Have the coordinator fork_session before invoking seo-audit so the performance-audit can run in the forked branch simultaneously. ❌ Incorrect.
fork_session is a mechanism for creating divergent exploration branches from a shared context, not a mechanism for parallelizing independent subagent tool calls within a single coordinator turn.

question: A logistics coordinator agent aggregates shipment status from three carrier APIs.
