Carrier A returns timestamps as Unix epoch integers and status codes 0/1, Carrier B returns ISO-8601 timestamps and the strings "OK"/"FAIL", and Carrier C returns "MM/DD/YYYY HH:mm" timestamps with the strings "DELIVERED"/"EXCEPTION". Over several weeks, the agent has occasionally misread a Carrier A status code of 0 as "delivered" instead of "exception," triggering incorrect customer notifications. What is the most effective way to prevent this class of error going forward?

choices.A: Add a paragraph to the agent's system prompt listing all three carriers' status code and timestamp conventions in detail so the model can convert them itself each time.
choices.B: Implement a PostToolUse hook that deterministically normalizes each carrier's raw response into a single consistent timestamp format and status vocabulary (e.g., mapping 0/"FAIL"/"EXCEPTION" all to a common "exception" value) before the model ever sees the tool result.
choices.C: Instruct the model, via the system prompt, to always double-check carrier status codes against a lookup table before reporting them to the customer.
choices.D: Ask the model to call each carrier API twice and average the two responses to reduce the chance of a misread.

explanation:
A. Add a paragraph to the agent's system prompt listing all three carriers' status code and timestamp conventions in detail so the model can convert them itself each time. ❌ Incorrect.
Relying on the model to correctly apply a set of conversion rules from a prompt on every single tool result still carries a non-zero failure rate, as the observed misreads demonstrate; the model can still occasionally slip on the conversion.
B. Implement a PostToolUse hook that deterministically normalizes each carrier's raw response into a single consistent timestamp format and status vocabulary (e.g., mapping 0/"FAIL"/"EXCEPTION" all to a common "exception" value) before the model ever sees the tool result. ✅ Correct.
A PostToolUse hook can transform heterogeneous raw data into a single normalized representation deterministically and mechanically, before the model has a chance to misinterpret carrier-specific conventions, which eliminates this entire class of interpretation error rather than just reducing its likelihood.
C. Instruct the model, via the system prompt, to always double-check carrier status codes against a lookup table before reporting them to the customer. ❌ Incorrect.
This is still a prompt-based instruction asking the model to perform the normalization itself on every call, which retains the same non-zero failure rate as option A rather than removing the ambiguity structurally.
D. Ask the model to call each carrier API twice and average the two responses to reduce the chance of a misread. ❌ Incorrect.
Averaging categorical status codes or repeated identical API calls does not address the root cause, which is inconsistent data formats, not transient noise in the underlying data.

question: A procurement agent's system prompt states: "Always route any purchase request above $10,000 to a human approver before executing." Across roughly 200 purchase requests handled over a month, the agent correctly escalated every request above the threshold except one: it directly approved and executed a $15,000 purchase order after reasoning that the vendor was "low-risk and previously approved," bypassing the required human review. What is the most effective way to guarantee this cannot happen again?

choices.A: Rewrite the system prompt to state the $10,000 threshold rule in bold, capitalized text and repeat it twice for emphasis.
choices.B: Lower the model's temperature setting so it reasons more conservatively about purchase risk.
choices.C: Implement a tool-call-interception hook that inspects the purchase amount before the purchase-execution tool call is allowed to run, and deterministically blocks and redirects to human escalation any purchase above $10,000, regardless of what the model's reasoning concluded.
choices.D: Add a follow-up confirmation step where the agent asks itself "are you sure this doesn't need escalation?" before executing any purchase.

explanation:
A. Rewrite the system prompt to state the $10,000 threshold rule in bold, capitalized text and repeat it twice for emphasis. ❌ Incorrect.
This is still a prompt-based control; formatting emphasis does not change the fact that prompt-based business-rule enforcement has a non-zero failure rate, as the one bypassed case already demonstrates even under a clearly stated rule.
B. Lower the model's temperature setting so it reasons more conservatively about purchase risk. ❌ Incorrect.
Temperature affects output variability broadly but provides no deterministic guarantee that a specific dollar-threshold rule will always be honored; it does not structurally prevent the bypass.
C. Implement a tool-call-interception hook that inspects the purchase amount before the purchase-execution tool call is allowed to run, and deterministically blocks and redirects to human escalation any purchase above $10,000, regardless of what the model's reasoning concluded. ✅ Correct.
A tool-call-interception hook enforces the threshold outside of the model's reasoning process entirely, so the $10,000 rule is guaranteed programmatically every single time, closing the gap that prompt-based guidance left open.
D. Add a follow-up confirmation step where the agent asks itself "are you sure this doesn't need escalation?" before executing any purchase. ❌ Incorrect.
A self-check performed by the same model using the same reasoning process is still prompt-based guidance and carries the same non-zero failure rate as the original instruction.

question: A billing-dispute agent handles a customer conversation for several minutes, making 8 tool calls to check account history, refund eligibility, and dispute status, before determining the case requires human review. It escalates by writing a single line into a queue: "Escalating case, please review." The human agent who picks up the case has no access to the original conversation transcript and only sees this one line, so they must ask the customer to repeat everything from scratch. What change to the escalation process would most effectively fix this?

choices.A: Have the agent escalate by dumping the full raw tool-call transcript from all 8 calls into the queue, unfiltered.
choices.B: Have the agent generate a structured handoff object containing key fields such as customer details, the root cause it identified, findings from relevant tool calls, and a recommended action, so the human agent has actionable context without needing transcript access.
choices.C: Instruct the agent, via its system prompt, to always be more verbose in its escalation message so the human agent can infer what happened.
choices.D: Reduce the number of tool calls the agent is allowed to make so escalations happen sooner and with less context to lose.

explanation:
A. Have the agent escalate by dumping the full raw tool-call transcript from all 8 calls into the queue, unfiltered. ❌ Incorrect.
A raw, unstructured transcript dump forces the human agent to manually sift through low-level tool call details under time pressure, which is slow and error-prone compared to a distilled, structured summary.
B. Have the agent generate a structured handoff object containing key fields such as customer details, the root cause it identified, findings from relevant tool calls, and a recommended action, so the human agent has actionable context without needing transcript access. ✅ Correct.
A structured handoff protocol packages exactly the information a human needs to act immediately — who the customer is, what the diagnosed root cause is, and what action is recommended — which is essential when the human has no transcript access at all.
C. Instruct the agent, via its system prompt, to always be more verbose in its escalation message so the human agent can infer what happened. ❌ Incorrect.
More verbosity without structure still leaves the human agent to parse free-form prose for the specific facts they need, which is slower and less reliable than clearly labeled structured fields.
D. Reduce the number of tool calls the agent is allowed to make so escalations happen sooner and with less context to lose. ❌ Incorrect.
Reducing tool calls limits the agent's ability to properly diagnose the issue before escalating and does not address the actual problem, which is the format of the handoff, not the amount of investigation performed.

question: A retail analytics team builds a coordinator to investigate "why churn rates are rising among mid-tier customers." The coordinator decomposes the work into a fixed, always-run three-step sequential chain: a demographics-agent, then a purchase-history-agent, then a geography-agent, each producing a report that feeds the next. The final synthesized report describes demographic and purchasing patterns in detail but completely misses that a competitor launched an aggressive promotion targeting the same segment last quarter and that support-ticket sentiment for that segment turned sharply negative around the same time — factors no subagent in the fixed chain was ever assigned to look at. What is the most effective way to address this gap?

choices.A: Add a fourth fixed step to the chain, a "competitor-agent," so the sequential pipeline now always covers four predetermined aspects instead of three.
choices.B: Keep the fixed three-step sequential chain, but instruct each subagent's system prompt to "consider any other relevant factors" before finishing its report.
choices.C: For this kind of open-ended investigative question, replace or supplement the fixed sequential decomposition with a dynamic, adaptive decomposition approach: have the coordinator first map the space of possible churn drivers, identify which areas look highest-impact based on early findings, and spin up additional subagents (e.g., competitive-intelligence, support-sentiment) as those signals emerge, rather than locking in a fixed set of aspects upfront.
choices.D: Reduce the investigation to a single generalist subagent that handles all aspects of the question in one pass, eliminating decomposition entirely.

explanation:
A. Add a fourth fixed step to the chain, a "competitor-agent," so the sequential pipeline now always covers four predetermined aspects instead of three. ❌ Incorrect.
This only patches the specific gap discovered this time; the next open-ended investigation may be missed by a different fixed aspect not anticipated in advance, since the core problem is relying on a predetermined, closed list of aspects for an inherently open-ended question.
B. Keep the fixed three-step sequential chain, but instruct each subagent's system prompt to "consider any other relevant factors" before finishing its report. ❌ Incorrect.
Each subagent is still scoped narrowly to its assigned aspect (demographics, purchase history, geography) and has no mechanism to actually go investigate an unassigned area like competitor promotions or support sentiment; a vague prompt addition doesn't expand what the subagent is actually equipped or directed to examine.
C. For this kind of open-ended investigative question, replace or supplement the fixed sequential decomposition with a dynamic, adaptive decomposition approach: have the coordinator first map the space of possible churn drivers, identify which areas look highest-impact based on early findings, and spin up additional subagents (e.g., competitive-intelligence, support-sentiment) as those signals emerge, rather than locking in a fixed set of aspects upfront. ✅ Correct.
Fixed sequential prompt chaining works well for predictable, multi-aspect reviews where the relevant aspects are known in advance, but an open-ended causal investigation like "why is churn rising" benefits from adaptive decomposition that can discover and pursue unanticipated high-impact areas such as competitor activity or sentiment shifts as evidence emerges.
D. Reduce the investigation to a single generalist subagent that handles all aspects of the question in one pass, eliminating decomposition entirely. ❌ Incorrect.
Eliminating decomposition sacrifices the benefits of specialized, focused analysis per aspect and does not solve the coverage problem; a single generalist pass is likely to be shallow across all aspects rather than adaptively deep where it matters.

question: A support-ticket triage agent has a `tag_ticket` tool. Product wants every incoming ticket to receive at least one tag, even if the model is unsure which one fits best. In testing, roughly 1 in 6 tickets comes back with a plain-text reply like "I'm not sure how to categorize this" instead of a tool call, leaving those tickets untagged in the queue. The team's current request sets `tool_choice: {"type": "auto"}`. What change fixes the untagged-ticket problem?

choices.A: Switch to `tool_choice: {"type": "any"}` so Claude must call some tool on every turn, guaranteeing a tag is always produced.
choices.B: Switch to `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "tag_ticket"}` so Claude is forced to call `tag_ticket` specifically on every turn.
choices.C: Keep `tool_choice: {"type": "auto"}` but add a second tool called `escalate_uncertain` so Claude has a fallback action.
choices.D: Remove `max_tokens` limits so Claude has room to reason before deciding whether to call the tool.

explanation:
A. Switch to `tool_choice: {"type": "any"}` so Claude must call some tool on every turn, guaranteeing a tag is always produced. ❌ Incorrect.
"any" only guarantees some tool call happens — if there is more than one tool available, Claude could call an unrelated one and still leave the ticket untagged in the way the team wants. It doesn't pin the specific tool needed.
B. Switch to `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "tag_ticket"}` so Claude is forced to call `tag_ticket` specifically on every turn. ✅ Correct.
Forcing the specific tool guarantees tag_ticket is invoked on every turn, eliminating the plain-text "I'm not sure" replies entirely. Since the requirement is literally "every ticket gets at least one tag," pinning the exact tool is the correct and simplest fix — the model can still express low confidence via a tool parameter (e.g. a confidence field) rather than by skipping the call.
C. Keep `tool_choice: {"type": "auto"}` but add a second tool called `escalate_uncertain` so Claude has a fallback action. ❌ Incorrect.
Under "auto" Claude can still choose to respond with plain text instead of calling either tool — adding a fallback tool doesn't change the underlying permissiveness of "auto", so untagged tickets can still occur.
D. Remove `max_tokens` limits so Claude has room to reason before deciding whether to call the tool. ❌ Incorrect.
max_tokens constraints on output length are unrelated to whether Claude decides to call a tool at all; this doesn't address the tool_choice setting causing the behavior.

question: An engineering-productivity team builds an agent that answers questions about a large Jira project (4,000+ issues). The only tool exposed is `search_issues(jql)`. Support logs show that for a simple question like "what epics exist in this project and how many open issues does each have," the agent issues 9-14 separate `search_issues` calls, incrementally discovering epic names one at a time and then querying each. The task takes over 40 seconds and burns a large number of tokens on repetitive JQL queries. What change addresses the root cause?

choices.A: Add a `list_all_issues` tool that returns every issue in the project in one call, so the agent has all data locally.
choices.B: Increase `max_tokens` so the agent can plan its JQL queries more carefully before issuing them.
choices.C: Expose an MCP resource (e.g. a project issue-summary/epic-hierarchy document) the agent can read up front, so it has the epic structure without needing exploratory search calls.
choices.D: Switch `tool_choice` from `"auto"` to `"any"` so the agent is forced to call a tool immediately instead of reasoning first.

explanation:
A. Add a `list_all_issues` tool that returns every issue in the project in one call, so the agent has all data locally. ❌ Incorrect.
Dumping 4,000+ issues into a single tool result would blow past context limits and still requires the model to parse an enormous unstructured payload — this is a tool-shaped fix for a problem that's actually about upfront visibility into a catalog, not fetching more raw data through the same action-oriented interface.
B. Increase `max_tokens` so the agent can plan its JQL queries more carefully before issuing them. ❌ Incorrect.
More output tokens don't give the agent knowledge it doesn't have — the repeated calls stem from not knowing what epics exist, not from insufficient room to reason.
C. Expose an MCP resource (e.g. a project issue-summary/epic-hierarchy document) the agent can read up front, so it has the epic structure without needing exploratory search calls. ✅ Correct.
MCP resources are designed exactly for this: static or slowly-changing catalogs (like an epic/issue hierarchy) that reduce exploratory tool-call chains. Reading one resource gives the agent the structure it was previously discovering through 9-14 rounds of search_issues, cutting latency and token spend dramatically.
D. Switch `tool_choice` from `"auto"` to `"any"` so the agent is forced to call a tool immediately instead of reasoning first. ❌ Incorrect.
Forcing a tool call doesn't reduce the number of calls needed — it only guarantees a tool is invoked each turn, which is not the bottleneck here.

question: A fintech startup runs Claude Code across two teams. Team A shares a custom MCP server (`ledger-mcp`) that wraps internal reconciliation APIs; every engineer on the team needs it, and it requires a `LEDGER_API_TOKEN` credential injected per-developer. Separately, one senior engineer, Priya, is prototyping a personal MCP server that talks to her own local Postgres sandbox for a side experiment unrelated to the team's work. The team lead wants `ledger-mcp` version-controlled and automatically available to every teammate who clones the repo, without ever committing the token, while Priya's experimental server should stay off everyone else's machine. How should this be configured?

choices.A: Put both `ledger-mcp` and Priya's Postgres server in the repo's `.mcp.json` with `${LEDGER_API_TOKEN}` expansion, and tell Priya to comment hers out before committing.
choices.B: Put `ledger-mcp` in project-level `.mcp.json` with `${LEDGER_API_TOKEN}` for credential expansion (committed, shared by the team), and have Priya add her Postgres server only to her own user-level `~/.claude.json`.
choices.C: Put both servers in `~/.claude.json` for every team member so each engineer can individually decide whether to enable them.
choices.D: Put `ledger-mcp` in `~/.claude.json` on the team lead's machine only, and have the team lead manually copy the config to each teammate's machine.

explanation:
A. Put both `ledger-mcp` and Priya's Postgres server in the repo's `.mcp.json` with `${LEDGER_API_TOKEN}` expansion, and tell Priya to comment hers out before committing. ❌ Incorrect.
This pollutes the shared, version-controlled project config with a personal experimental server, risking accidental commits and confusing every other teammate who opens the repo — project .mcp.json should only contain team-wide tooling.
B. Put `ledger-mcp` in project-level `.mcp.json` with `${LEDGER_API_TOKEN}` for credential expansion (committed, shared by the team), and have Priya add her Postgres server only to her own user-level `~/.claude.json`. ✅ Correct.
This is exactly the intended split: .mcp.json is checked into the repo so every teammate automatically gets ledger-mcp, with ${LEDGER_API_TOKEN} expansion meaning each developer's own environment variable is substituted rather than a secret being committed. Priya's personal, experimental, unrelated server belongs in her own ~/.claude.json, which is never shared or version-controlled.
C. Put both servers in `~/.claude.json` for every team member so each engineer can individually decide whether to enable them. ❌ Incorrect.
This defeats the purpose of a shared team tool — new engineers wouldn't automatically get ledger-mcp when they clone the repo, and the team would need an out-of-band process to keep everyone's personal config in sync.
D. Put `ledger-mcp` in `~/.claude.json` on the team lead's machine only, and have the team lead manually copy the config to each teammate's machine. ❌ Incorrect.
Manual copying is exactly what project-level .mcp.json exists to eliminate; it doesn't scale, isn't version-controlled, and provides no clean mechanism for per-developer credential injection.

question: A platform team is adding Slack integration to their internal agent so it can post deployment summaries to channels and look up teammate profiles. An engineer proposes writing a custom MCP server that wraps Slack's Web API (`chat.postMessage`, `users.info`, etc.) from scratch, estimating two weeks of work plus ongoing maintenance for pagination, rate-limit handling, and auth refresh. What is the better architectural decision?

choices.A: Build the custom server as proposed — custom MCP servers give the team full control over the tool surface and are worth the investment even for standard integrations.
choices.B: Skip MCP entirely and have the agent call Slack's REST API directly via a generic HTTP tool, since that avoids the MCP protocol overhead.
choices.C: Adopt an existing, actively-maintained community/vendor Slack MCP server for the standard messaging and lookup operations, and reserve custom-server effort for anything genuinely team-specific (e.g. a bespoke internal approval workflow layered on top).
choices.D: Build the custom server, but only implement `chat.postMessage` and skip `users.info` to reduce scope.

explanation:
A. Build the custom server as proposed — custom MCP servers give the team full control over the tool surface and are worth the investment even for standard integrations. ❌ Incorrect.
Full control isn't valuable here because Slack's messaging and lookup APIs are a standard integration with well-established community/vendor MCP servers already handling pagination, rate limits, and OAuth refresh — reinventing that is wasted engineering effort with ongoing maintenance cost.
B. Skip MCP entirely and have the agent call Slack's REST API directly via a generic HTTP tool, since that avoids the MCP protocol overhead. ❌ Incorrect.
This throws away the benefits of MCP's standardized tool/resource interface and still leaves the team responsible for hand-rolling auth, pagination, and error handling — it doesn't reduce effort, it just moves the same problem to a different layer.
C. Adopt an existing, actively-maintained community/vendor Slack MCP server for the standard messaging and lookup operations, and reserve custom-server effort for anything genuinely team-specific (e.g. a bespoke internal approval workflow layered on top). ✅ Correct.
This is the correct default: for standard, well-defined integrations like Slack, Jira, or GitHub, an existing MCP server already solves the hard problems (auth, pagination, rate limiting) and is maintained by others. Reserve custom MCP server development for workflows that are genuinely specific to the team and not served by any existing integration.
D. Build the custom server, but only implement `chat.postMessage` and skip `users.info` to reduce scope. ❌ Incorrect.
This still commits to building and maintaining a custom server for a standard integration; reducing scope doesn't fix the underlying decision to reinvent something that already exists off-the-shelf.

question: A codebase has a `formatCurrency` utility used across an e-commerce checkout flow. An agent is asked: "Find every place that calls `formatCurrency` and check whether it handles negative amounts correctly." A `grep -r "formatCurrency"` search turns up only 6 call sites, but a later code-review reveals at least 20 places actually depend on this formatting logic, because the function is re-exported under aliases (`fmtMoney`, `priceDisplay`) from two different wrapper modules, and those aliases are what most callers actually import. What is the correct approach for the agent to reliably find every caller?

choices.A: Increase the grep search to be case-insensitive and repeat it, since the aliases are probably just differently-cased versions of the same name.
choices.B: First identify every exported name that resolves to `formatCurrency` (including re-exports and aliases from wrapper modules), then run a separate search for each of those names across the codebase.
choices.C: Use Glob to find all `*.ts` files in the repo and manually open each one to check for currency formatting logic.
choices.D: Trust the original grep result of 6 call sites, since `formatCurrency` is the canonical name and any code not using it directly is out of scope for the review.

explanation:
A. Increase the grep search to be case-insensitive and repeat it, since the aliases are probably just differently-cased versions of the same name. ❌ Incorrect.
fmtMoney and priceDisplay are not case variants of formatCurrency — they are distinct exported names introduced by wrapper modules, so no amount of case-insensitivity in the search will surface them.
B. First identify every exported name that resolves to `formatCurrency` (including re-exports and aliases from wrapper modules), then run a separate search for each of those names across the codebase. ✅ Correct.
Because wrapper modules re-export the function under different names, a single grep for the original symbol misses most real callers. The reliable approach is to first trace the module graph to enumerate every exported alias (fmtMoney, priceDisplay, etc.), then search for each alias independently — only this two-step process surfaces all ~20 actual call sites.
C. Use Glob to find all `*.ts` files in the repo and manually open each one to check for currency formatting logic. ❌ Incorrect.
Glob finds files by path pattern, not by content, and manually opening every file in a large codebase doesn't scale and isn't targeted — it also doesn't solve the actual problem, which is not knowing the aliases to search for.
D. Trust the original grep result of 6 call sites, since `formatCurrency` is the canonical name and any code not using it directly is out of scope for the review. ❌ Incorrect.
This directly contradicts the task's goal of finding every place the formatting logic is actually used; ignoring re-exported aliases means the review misses the majority of real dependents and gives a false sense of completeness.

question: A team connects an internal `codesearch-mcp` server that performs semantic, embedding-based code search across a 2-million-line monorepo — it can answer queries like "find the function that validates webhook signatures" even when the exact string doesn't appear anywhere. Its tool description currently reads: `"search_code: Searches the codebase. Input: query (string)."` In practice, the coding agent almost always reaches for the built-in Grep tool instead, even for exactly the semantic queries `search_code` was built to handle, and only falls back to `search_code` after several failed Grep attempts. What is the most effective fix?

choices.A: Remove the built-in Grep tool from the agent's toolset entirely so `search_code` is the only text-search option available.
choices.B: Rewrite the `search_code` tool description to explain its semantic/embedding-based capability and give concrete examples of the kind of natural-language or intent-based queries where it outperforms literal string search, e.g. "finding code by what it does, not just its exact text."
choices.C: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "search_code"}` for every request so the agent is forced to call it first.
choices.D: Rename the tool from `search_code` to `grep` so the agent associates it with its existing search habits.

explanation:
A. Remove the built-in Grep tool from the agent's toolset entirely so `search_code` is the only text-search option available. ❌ Incorrect.
This is a blunt workaround that also removes Grep's legitimate use cases (fast literal/regex search), rather than fixing the actual problem, which is that the MCP tool's description doesn't communicate when it's the better choice.
B. Rewrite the `search_code` tool description to explain its semantic/embedding-based capability and give concrete examples of the kind of natural-language or intent-based queries where it outperforms literal string search, e.g. "finding code by what it does, not just its exact text." ✅ Correct.
Tool descriptions are the primary signal Claude uses to select between overlapping tools. A minimal description ("Searches the codebase") gives the model no reason to prefer this MCP tool over the familiar built-in Grep. Rewriting the description to explicitly convey the tool's unique semantic capability and give example trigger scenarios is the standard, effective fix for exactly this under-description problem.
C. Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "search_code"}` for every request so the agent is forced to call it first. ❌ Incorrect.
Forcing this one tool on every request would break any task that doesn't need code search at all, and doesn't scale to a toolset with multiple tools — tool_choice isn't the mechanism for teaching the model when a tool is the better fit among alternatives.
D. Rename the tool from `search_code` to `grep` so the agent associates it with its existing search habits. ❌ Incorrect.
Renaming to match the built-in tool's name would create ambiguity and doesn't communicate the tool's actual semantic capability — it addresses naming, not the substance of what the tool does or when to prefer it.

question: A `get_account_documents(account_id)` MCP tool queries a document-storage backend for files attached to a customer account. It returns `isError: false` with an empty `content` array both when an account genuinely has no documents on file, and — due to a backend bug — when the caller's service credentials lack permission to view that account's documents at all. Support engineers report that the agent frequently tells customers "you have no documents on file" when in fact the documents exist but the underlying integration user was denied access. What change to the tool's output most directly fixes this?

choices.A: Change the tool to always set `isError: true` whenever `content` is empty, regardless of the reason, so the agent knows something went wrong.
choices.B: Have the tool distinguish the two cases in its result: return a genuinely empty, successful result for "no documents exist," but return `isError: true` with structured metadata (e.g. `errorCategory: "permission"`, `isRetryable: false`) when the query fails due to an access/permission problem.
choices.C: Leave `isError` and the empty-array behavior unchanged, but add a line to the system prompt instructing the agent to always double-check with the customer before reporting zero documents.
choices.D: Reduce the tool's timeout so permission failures happen faster, giving the agent more time budget to retry.

explanation:
A. Change the tool to always set `isError: true` whenever `content` is empty, regardless of the reason, so the agent knows something went wrong. ❌ Incorrect.
This conflates the two cases in the opposite direction — now a customer who legitimately has zero documents would be reported as an error, which is just as wrong as the original bug and gives the agent no way to tell the two apart.
B. Have the tool distinguish the two cases in its result: return a genuinely empty, successful result for "no documents exist," but return `isError: true` with structured metadata (e.g. `errorCategory: "permission"`, `isRetryable: false`) when the query fails due to an access/permission problem. ✅ Correct.
The root cause is that a valid empty result and an access failure are indistinguishable in the tool's current output. Giving each its own signal — a clean empty success for the former, and isError: true with errorCategory/isRetryable metadata for the latter — lets the agent correctly tell the customer "no documents found" only when that's actually true, and otherwise surface or escalate a permission problem rather than mis-reporting it as an empty result.
C. Leave `isError` and the empty-array behavior unchanged, but add a line to the system prompt instructing the agent to always double-check with the customer before reporting zero documents. ❌ Incorrect.
Prompting around a missing signal doesn't give the agent any way to actually detect the permission failure — the tool's output still contains no information distinguishing the two cases, so the agent has nothing reliable to act on.
D. Reduce the tool's timeout so permission failures happen faster, giving the agent more time budget to retry. ❌ Incorrect.
Timeout tuning is unrelated to the actual defect, which is a missing distinction between success and failure in the tool's result shape, not a latency problem.

question: A logistics company's dispatch agent has a `dispatch_driver(order_id, driver_id)` tool that must never be called without human confirmation, because an accidental dispatch sends a real driver to a real address. The system prompt says: "Always ask the user to confirm before calling dispatch_driver." During a post-incident review, the team finds a transcript where Claude, mid-conversation, went straight from "Let me dispatch a driver for this" directly into a `dispatch_driver` tool call with no confirmation step, despite `tool_choice` being left at its default. What is the most reliable fix?

choices.A: Reword the system prompt to say "CRITICAL: YOU MUST ALWAYS confirm with the user before calling dispatch_driver, no exceptions" in all caps for emphasis.
choices.B: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "dispatch_driver"}` so the model is forced to call it immediately, removing ambiguity about when it fires.
choices.C: Remove reliance on prompt instructions alone for this safety boundary — gate the dispatch action in the harness itself (e.g. requiring a separate explicit confirmation tool call or a human-approval step before the dispatch tool actually executes), since prompting cannot guarantee tool-call behavior.
choices.D: Switch `tool_choice` to `{"type": "none"}` whenever `dispatch_driver` is in the toolset, so Claude can never call any tool without the user typing the tool name manually.

explanation:
A. Reword the system prompt to say "CRITICAL: YOU MUST ALWAYS confirm with the user before calling dispatch_driver, no exceptions" in all caps for emphasis. ❌ Incorrect.
Stronger prompt wording can reduce the frequency of the failure but is still just a prompt — it provides no hard guarantee, and for an action with real-world physical consequences (dispatching a driver), a probabilistic safeguard is not sufficient.
B. Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "dispatch_driver"}` so the model is forced to call it immediately, removing ambiguity about when it fires. ❌ Incorrect.
This makes the problem strictly worse — forcing the dispatch tool to be called removes the model's ability to not call it, guaranteeing a dispatch happens on every turn rather than adding any confirmation step.
C. Remove reliance on prompt instructions alone for this safety boundary — gate the dispatch action in the harness itself (e.g. requiring a separate explicit confirmation tool call or a human-approval step before the dispatch tool actually executes), since prompting cannot guarantee tool-call behavior. ✅ Correct.
For actions with real, hard-to-reverse consequences, the confirmation boundary must be enforced structurally — in the harness/tool-execution layer — rather than relying on the model reliably following a prompt instruction every time. This is the standard pattern for tamper-proof safety gates: the model can request the action, but the harness requires a separate confirmation signal before actually executing it, regardless of what the system prompt says.
D. Switch `tool_choice` to `{"type": "none"}` whenever `dispatch_driver` is in the toolset, so Claude can never call any tool without the user typing the tool name manually. ❌ Incorrect.
{"type": "none"} disables all tool calls, not just dispatch_driver — this breaks every other legitimate tool the dispatch agent needs and does not provide a working confirmation mechanism for dispatch specifically.

question: A platform team's monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md with company-wide engineering standards. The services/payments/ directory contains a payments-domain-rules.md file with detailed domain-specific validation rules that only engineers working in that service need to see, and that would add unnecessary context weight for everyone else. What is the best way to make Claude load these domain rules automatically only when working inside services/payments/, without duplicating the content into the root CLAUDE.md?

choices.A: Paste the contents of payments-domain-rules.md directly into the root CLAUDE.md so it is always available everywhere.
choices.B: Create a services/payments/CLAUDE.md that includes an @import line referencing payments-domain-rules.md, relying on directory-level CLAUDE.md discovery plus @import for modular inclusion.
choices.C: Rename payments-domain-rules.md to CLAUDE.md and place an identical copy in every subdirectory under services/payments/.
choices.D: Add payments-domain-rules.md as an entry under allowed-tools in .claude/settings.json so Claude reads it before editing files in that directory.

explanation:
A. Paste the contents of payments-domain-rules.md directly into the root CLAUDE.md so it is always available everywhere. ❌ Incorrect.
This loads the domain-specific rules into every session regardless of where the engineer is working, adding context weight for people who never touch payments — the opposite of what the team wants.
B. Create a services/payments/CLAUDE.md that includes an @import line referencing payments-domain-rules.md, relying on directory-level CLAUDE.md discovery plus @import for modular inclusion. ✅ Correct.
Claude Code discovers CLAUDE.md files up the directory tree from the working location, and @import lets a directory-level CLAUDE.md pull in a separate standards file without copy-pasting its contents, so the rules load automatically only when working in or under services/payments/.
C. Rename payments-domain-rules.md to CLAUDE.md and place an identical copy in every subdirectory under services/payments/. ❌ Incorrect.
This creates exactly the duplication-with-no-single-source-of-truth problem the team is trying to avoid; edits would need to be propagated manually to every copy.
D. Add payments-domain-rules.md as an entry under allowed-tools in .claude/settings.json so Claude reads it before editing files in that directory. ❌ Incorrect.
allowed-tools governs which tools a skill or session may invoke, not which memory files get loaded into context; it has no mechanism for including standards documents.

question: A senior engineer maintained a personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md with rules like 'always write commit messages in Conventional Commits format' and 'run make lint before finishing.' When a new teammate joins and starts a fresh Claude Code session in the same repository, none of those rules apply to their sessions even though the senior engineer insists the rules are 'in CLAUDE.md.' What is the most likely cause and correct fix?

choices.A: The rules are stored in the senior engineer's user-level CLAUDE.md, which is personal and not checked into version control, so the new teammate's machine never loads them; the fix is to move the shared rules into the project's ./CLAUDE.md so it's committed and loaded for everyone.
choices.B: CLAUDE.md rules expire after a fixed number of sessions and must be periodically refreshed by every engineer individually.
choices.C: The new teammate needs to run /memory in every new terminal tab before any CLAUDE.md rules take effect.
choices.D: Project-level CLAUDE.md files only apply to whichever engineer authored the most recent commit that touched them.

explanation:
A. The rules are stored in the senior engineer's user-level CLAUDE.md, which is personal and not checked into version control, so the new teammate's machine never loads them; the fix is to move the shared rules into the project's ./CLAUDE.md so it's committed and loaded for everyone. ✅ Correct.
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is scoped to that individual user's machine and is never shared through the repository, so any teammate-facing convention placed there silently fails to propagate; moving it into the project's committed CLAUDE.md fixes this for the whole team.
B. CLAUDE.md rules expire after a fixed number of sessions and must be periodically refreshed by every engineer individually. ❌ Incorrect.
There is no expiration mechanism for CLAUDE.md content; files are simply re-read at session start based on hierarchy, not on a timer.
C. The new teammate needs to run /memory in every new terminal tab before any CLAUDE.md rules take effect. ❌ Incorrect.
/memory is a read-only diagnostic that shows which memory files are currently loaded; it does not load or activate anything itself.
D. Project-level CLAUDE.md files only apply to whichever engineer authored the most recent commit that touched them. ❌ Incorrect.
Project CLAUDE.md content applies to any session working in that repository regardless of authorship; there is no author-based restriction.

question: A team builds a .claude/skills/db-cleanup/SKILL.md skill that connects to a staging database to find and report orphaned records for manual review. During testing, the skill occasionally attempts to run DELETE statements directly instead of just reporting findings, which the team wants to prevent entirely regardless of how the prompt is worded. Which SKILL.md frontmatter field should they configure, and how?

choices.A: Set argument-hint to a string warning that deletions are not allowed, so Claude is reminded not to delete records when the skill is invoked.
choices.B: Set context: fork so the skill's exploratory database queries run in an isolated conversation context, separate from the main session.
choices.C: Set allowed-tools to restrict the skill to read-only/reporting tools, excluding any tool capable of executing destructive SQL, so destructive actions are blocked at the tool-access level regardless of prompt wording.
choices.D: Rename the skill file to include 'readonly' in its name so Claude treats it as non-destructive by convention.

explanation:
A. Set argument-hint to a string warning that deletions are not allowed, so Claude is reminded not to delete records when the skill is invoked. ❌ Incorrect.
argument-hint only prompts the user for required parameters when invoking a skill; it has no effect on which actions or tools Claude is permitted to use during execution.
B. Set context: fork so the skill's exploratory database queries run in an isolated conversation context, separate from the main session. ❌ Incorrect.
context: fork isolates verbose or exploratory output from polluting the main conversation; it does not restrict which tools or actions the skill can take, so destructive statements could still run.
C. Set allowed-tools to restrict the skill to read-only/reporting tools, excluding any tool capable of executing destructive SQL, so destructive actions are blocked at the tool-access level regardless of prompt wording. ✅ Correct.
allowed-tools is enforced by the harness itself, not by the model's interpretation of instructions, so it reliably prevents a skill from invoking tools capable of destructive operations even if the prompt is ambiguous or Claude misjudges intent.
D. Rename the skill file to include 'readonly' in its name so Claude treats it as non-destructive by convention. ❌ Incorrect.
File naming has no enforcement power over tool access; it is purely cosmetic and does not stop destructive tool calls from being attempted.

question: The project ships a shared .claude/skills/standup-notes/SKILL.md that formats daily standup notes in a template the whole team agreed on. One engineer wants a personal variant that adds an extra 'blockers I need help with' section for her own use, but she doesn't want to modify the shared project skill or risk affecting how her teammates' sessions behave. What should she do?

choices.A: Edit .claude/skills/standup-notes/SKILL.md directly and add her section, since Skills are always scoped per user even when stored in the project.
choices.B: Create a new skill under a different name, e.g. ~/.claude/skills/standup-notes-mine/SKILL.md, with her customized template; this personal, user-level skill exists only in her own configuration and doesn't touch the shared project skill or her teammates' sessions.
choices.C: Copy the shared skill into a new project directory, .claude/skills/standup-notes-v2/, and commit it so teammates can opt in later.
choices.D: Add a personal override block inside the shared SKILL.md's frontmatter that activates only when her username is detected.

explanation:
A. Edit .claude/skills/standup-notes/SKILL.md directly and add her section, since Skills are always scoped per user even when stored in the project. ❌ Incorrect.
Skills stored in the project's .claude/skills/ are shared and version-controlled, so editing the file in place changes it for the whole team, not just her.
B. Create a new skill under a different name, e.g. ~/.claude/skills/standup-notes-mine/SKILL.md, with her customized template; this personal, user-level skill exists only in her own configuration and doesn't touch the shared project skill or her teammates' sessions. ✅ Correct.
Personal skills placed in ~/.claude/skills/ under a distinct name live entirely in the individual's own user-level configuration, leaving the shared project skill untouched and invisible to the change for everyone else.
C. Copy the shared skill into a new project directory, .claude/skills/standup-notes-v2/, and commit it so teammates can opt in later. ❌ Incorrect.
This still commits the variant to the shared project, affecting the repository and every teammate who pulls it, which is the opposite of what she wants.
D. Add a personal override block inside the shared SKILL.md's frontmatter that activates only when her username is detected. ❌ Incorrect.
SKILL.md frontmatter has no per-user conditional activation mechanism; this isn't a supported feature.

question: A team's project CLAUDE.md states: 'All new database migrations must include a corresponding rollback script in the same PR.' Reviewers notice this rule is consistently followed for migrations added under apps/api/migrations/ but is frequently missing for migrations added under apps/reporting/migrations/, even though both are subdirectories of the same repository and both teams claim to use the same base Claude Code setup. What is the most efficient first step to diagnose why the rule isn't reaching sessions working in apps/reporting/?

choices.A: Ask the reporting team to manually retype the rollback-script rule into their prompt every single time they generate a migration.
choices.B: Run the /memory command while working in apps/reporting/ to see exactly which CLAUDE.md files are actually being loaded for that directory, then compare against what loads for apps/api/.
choices.C: Delete the root CLAUDE.md and rewrite it from scratch, assuming the file has become corrupted.
choices.D: Switch the reporting team to plan mode, since plan mode is required for CLAUDE.md rules to take effect.

explanation:
A. Ask the reporting team to manually retype the rollback-script rule into their prompt every single time they generate a migration. ❌ Incorrect.
This is a manual workaround that doesn't diagnose the root cause and would need to be repeated indefinitely rather than fixing the configuration.
B. Run the /memory command while working in apps/reporting/ to see exactly which CLAUDE.md files are actually being loaded for that directory, then compare against what loads for apps/api/. ✅ Correct.
/memory shows the concrete set of memory files loaded for the current working location, making it the fastest way to reveal whether apps/reporting/ has a conflicting or missing directory-level CLAUDE.md, or whether the root file simply isn't being picked up there, versus what's loading for apps/api/.
C. Delete the root CLAUDE.md and rewrite it from scratch, assuming the file has become corrupted. ❌ Incorrect.
This is destructive and unjustified without first confirming the file is even the problem; it also discards working configuration for apps/api/.
D. Switch the reporting team to plan mode, since plan mode is required for CLAUDE.md rules to take effect. ❌ Incorrect.
CLAUDE.md rules load regardless of plan mode versus direct execution; plan mode has no bearing on which memory files are read.

question: An architect is using plan mode to redesign a permissions system that touches authentication, authorization middleware, and five API route modules. Before proposing a plan, they need to survey how permission checks are currently implemented across roughly 40 files, but they're worried that dumping all of that exploratory file-reading and note-taking into the main conversation will exhaust context before the planning and implementation phases even start. What's the best approach?

choices.A: Read all 40 files directly in the main conversation so the architect has full visibility into every detail before planning.
choices.B: Skip exploration entirely and let Claude propose a plan based on file names alone.
choices.C: Dispatch the discovery work to an Explore subagent, which reads and digests the ~40 files in its own isolated context and returns a condensed summary, keeping the main conversation's context free for the planning and implementation phases that follow.
choices.D: Split the task into 40 separate plan-mode sessions, one per file, and manually merge the resulting plans afterward.

explanation:
A. Read all 40 files directly in the main conversation so the architect has full visibility into every detail before planning. ❌ Incorrect.
This is exactly the approach that risks exhausting the main context before planning and implementation even begin, since every file's full contents accumulate in the same conversation.
B. Skip exploration entirely and let Claude propose a plan based on file names alone. ❌ Incorrect.
A multi-file architectural change like this needs actual understanding of current implementation patterns; skipping exploration risks a plan that misses existing conventions or edge cases.
C. Dispatch the discovery work to an Explore subagent, which reads and digests the ~40 files in its own isolated context and returns a condensed summary, keeping the main conversation's context free for the planning and implementation phases that follow. ✅ Correct.
The Explore subagent is designed precisely for this: it performs the verbose, exploratory reading in its own context and reports back a distilled summary, protecting the main conversation from context exhaustion during a multi-phase task.
D. Split the task into 40 separate plan-mode sessions, one per file, and manually merge the resulting plans afterward. ❌ Incorrect.
This fragments a cohesive architectural change into disconnected per-file plans and creates significant manual overhead reconciling them, without addressing the underlying context problem.

question: A CI pipeline currently runs Claude Code in print mode to review PR diffs, then uses a fragile set of regex patterns to scrape the plain-text output before posting inline PR comments. The regex parser regularly breaks when Claude phrases findings slightly differently between runs. The team wants a reliable, machine-parseable way to extract findings such as file path, line number, severity, and message. What should they change?

choices.A: Ask Claude in the prompt to always format findings using a specific Markdown table layout, and keep using regex to parse that table.
choices.B: Invoke Claude Code with --output-format json combined with --json-schema describing the desired findings structure, so Claude returns structured JSON conforming to that schema, which the pipeline can deserialize directly instead of regex-scraping prose.
choices.C: Increase the CI job's timeout so Claude has more time to format its plain-text output consistently.
choices.D: Switch from print mode to interactive mode so a human can manually copy findings into PR comments.

explanation:
A. Ask Claude in the prompt to always format findings using a specific Markdown table layout, and keep using regex to parse that table. ❌ Incorrect.
Prose-based formatting instructions are still natural language output subject to phrasing drift; regex parsing against it remains brittle even with a requested table layout.
B. Invoke Claude Code with --output-format json combined with --json-schema describing the desired findings structure, so Claude returns structured JSON conforming to that schema, which the pipeline can deserialize directly instead of regex-scraping prose. ✅ Correct.
--output-format json paired with --json-schema constrains the response to a defined structure with typed fields like file, line, severity, and message, giving the pipeline a stable, directly-deserializable contract instead of fragile text scraping.
C. Increase the CI job's timeout so Claude has more time to format its plain-text output consistently. ❌ Incorrect.
More time does not make free-form text output structurally consistent; the fragility comes from parsing prose, not from insufficient time.
D. Switch from print mode to interactive mode so a human can manually copy findings into PR comments. ❌ Incorrect.
This defeats the purpose of automated CI review by reintroducing manual work, and interactive mode isn't suited to a non-interactive pipeline anyway.

question: A PR review bot runs an AI code review on every push. On the first push, it posted five inline comments. The author fixed three issues and pushed two new commits. On the re-run, the bot re-reviews the full diff from scratch and posts all five original findings again as new comments, including the three that were already fixed, cluttering the PR. What change to the review workflow would prevent this duplication?

choices.A: Have the bot skip review entirely on any push that isn't the very first one.
choices.B: Increase the bot's confidence threshold so it reports fewer findings overall on every run.
choices.C: Include the prior review's findings, and which ones were addressed, in the context passed to the re-run, and instruct Claude to report only issues that are new or still unaddressed rather than restating everything it already found.
choices.D: Delete the previous PR comments before each re-run so the PR has a clean visual slate, without changing what is passed into the review prompt.

explanation:
A. Have the bot skip review entirely on any push that isn't the very first one. ❌ Incorrect.
This would miss reviewing genuinely new issues introduced by later commits, trading duplication for blind spots rather than solving the actual problem.
B. Increase the bot's confidence threshold so it reports fewer findings overall on every run. ❌ Incorrect.
A higher confidence threshold addresses precision/recall trade-offs, not duplication; the bot would still re-report the same high-confidence issues on every re-run since it has no memory of what was fixed.
C. Include the prior review's findings, and which ones were addressed, in the context passed to the re-run, and instruct Claude to report only issues that are new or still unaddressed rather than restating everything it already found. ✅ Correct.
Giving Claude visibility into what was previously flagged and fixed lets it compare against the current diff and report only the delta, which directly eliminates re-posting findings that have already been resolved.
D. Delete the previous PR comments before each re-run so the PR has a clean visual slate, without changing what is passed into the review prompt. ❌ Incorrect.
This only hides the visual clutter of old comments; without changing the prompt context, the bot still has no awareness of what was fixed and will simply regenerate all five findings again.

question: A developer uses a long-running Claude Code session to implement a fairly involved caching feature across several files. Once finished, they ask the same session to 'review this change for bugs and edge cases' before opening a PR. The review comes back with only minor style comments and misses a real cache-invalidation bug that a teammate later catches by eye. Why is the same session that wrote the code generally a weaker reviewer of that code than a fresh, independent review instance would be?

choices.A: Claude Code's review quality mechanically degrades over time purely due to elapsed token usage, so any sufficiently long session reviews poorly regardless of content.
choices.B: The session has already committed to the design decisions and rationale it used while writing the code, so when asked to review, it tends to re-confirm its own prior reasoning and assumptions rather than critically re-examining them from scratch; the same blind spots that led to the bug in the first place carry over into the review.
choices.C: The same session cannot access the file system to re-read the changed files, so it reviews entirely from memory of what it wrote.
choices.D: Print mode must be used for any self-review to succeed, and interactive sessions are structurally incapable of reviewing code they generated.

explanation:
A. Claude Code's review quality mechanically degrades over time purely due to elapsed token usage, so any sufficiently long session reviews poorly regardless of content. ❌ Incorrect.
The issue isn't a generic degradation from session length; a long session reviewing unrelated code it didn't write doesn't suffer the same self-review blind spot, so length alone isn't the mechanism.
B. The session has already committed to the design decisions and rationale it used while writing the code, so when asked to review, it tends to re-confirm its own prior reasoning and assumptions rather than critically re-examining them from scratch; the same blind spots that led to the bug in the first place carry over into the review. ✅ Correct.
Because the session's context already contains its own justification for the design choices it made, reviewing 'in place' tends to reinforce those same assumptions rather than challenge them, which is why an independent instance with no prior investment in the implementation is more likely to catch issues like the missed invalidation edge case.
C. The same session cannot access the file system to re-read the changed files, so it reviews entirely from memory of what it wrote. ❌ Incorrect.
The session can re-read files just as well as any other session; file access isn't the limiting factor here.
D. Print mode must be used for any self-review to succeed, and interactive sessions are structurally incapable of reviewing code they generated. ❌ Incorrect.
Interactive sessions are fully capable of running a review; the mode of invocation isn't what causes the weaker self-review, the shared prior context and reasoning is.

question: A finance team built a Claude-based expense report auditor with this instruction: "Flag any expense report that looks unusual or excessive, but be conservative — only flag things you are highly confident about." After one month, auditors discover the tool missed a pattern where employees split a $140 dinner into two $70 charges submitted a day apart to dodge the $75 itemized-receipt requirement, while also flagging several legitimate $600 conference travel bundles as "excessive." What change would most directly fix this?

choices.A: Reword the instruction to "Be extremely conservative — only flag a report if you are more than 95% certain it violates policy."
choices.B: Replace the vague criteria with an explicit, enumerated list of violation conditions and thresholds, such as "flag if two or more transactions from the same vendor within 48 hours sum to more than $75" and "flag meals over $100/day without an itemized receipt."
choices.C: Lower the model's temperature setting so its flagging decisions are more deterministic.
choices.D: Add a second pass where the model reviews its own flagged list before returning results.

explanation:
A. Reword the instruction to be "extremely conservative" with a confidence percentage. ❌ Incorrect.
This is still a vague qualitative instruction — "95% certain" isn't a criterion the model can evaluate against concrete policy rules, so it doesn't address why split transactions were missed or why legitimate bundles were flagged.
B. Replace the vague criteria with an explicit, enumerated list of violation conditions and thresholds. ✅ Correct.
Vague terms like "unusual," "excessive," and "conservative" leave the model to guess at intent, causing both missed violations (split transactions with no defined threshold) and false flags (large but legitimate travel costs). Concrete, categorical criteria — specific dollar thresholds, time windows, and vendor-matching rules — give the model an unambiguous decision procedure that catches the split-transaction pattern while sparing legitimate expenses.
C. Lower the model's temperature setting. ❌ Incorrect.
Temperature affects output randomness, not the model's underlying understanding of what counts as a violation; the root cause is underspecified criteria, not inconsistency between runs.
D. Add a self-review pass over the model's own flagged list. ❌ Incorrect.
A self-review pass in the same context still applies the same vague criteria and reasoning, so it wouldn't reliably catch violations the model didn't understand were violations in the first place.

question: An insurer uses Claude to triage incoming auto claims into "routine," "needs_adjuster," or "possible_fraud." The initial prompt included five few-shot examples, all clean, clearly worded claims with obvious classifications. After launch, claims written in informal, contradictory, or sarcastic language (e.g., "car is 'fine,' just missing a door and on fire") are frequently misclassified as "routine." What change to the few-shot examples would best help the model generalize correctly to new, unseen ambiguous claims like this?

choices.A: Add many more clean, unambiguous examples covering additional routine claim types.
choices.B: Include a few examples of genuinely ambiguous or contradictory claims paired with the reasoning that leads to the correct classification, rather than only clear-cut cases.
choices.C: Remove the few-shot examples entirely and rely on the enum definition alone to constrain output.
choices.D: Increase the max_tokens parameter so the model has more room to describe the claim.

explanation:
A. Add many more clean, unambiguous examples of additional routine claim types. ❌ Incorrect.
More examples of the same easy pattern reinforce what the model already handles well but do nothing to teach it how to reason through contradictory or informally worded claims, which is where the errors are occurring.
B. Include a few examples of ambiguous or contradictory claims paired with the reasoning that leads to the correct classification. ✅ Correct.
Few-shot examples that demonstrate how to resolve ambiguity — not just memorized surface patterns — teach the model a generalizable judgment process. This lets it correctly handle novel ambiguous claims it has never seen, rather than only matching claims that closely resemble the training examples.
C. Remove the few-shot examples entirely and rely on the enum definition alone. ❌ Incorrect.
An enum only constrains the output format to valid category labels; it provides no guidance on how to judge ambiguous or contradictory content, so removing the examples would make the ambiguity problem worse, not better.
D. Increase the max_tokens parameter. ❌ Incorrect.
This is a classification task with a short categorical output; token budget is not the limiting factor and has no bearing on the model's ability to interpret ambiguous claim language.

question: A document-processing pipeline ingests scanned files that could be invoices, receipts, or purchase orders — the type is not known before processing. Three tools are defined: extract_invoice, extract_receipt, and extract_purchase_order, each with a schema tailored to that document type. The team wants a guarantee that every request results in a structured tool call using whichever schema actually fits the document, and never a plain conversational text response. Which tool_choice configuration achieves this?

choices.A: tool_choice: "auto", allowing the model to decide whether to call a tool or respond in text.
choices.B: tool_choice: "any", requiring the model to call one of the three tools but letting it pick which one fits the document.
choices.C: tool_choice forced to a specific tool, such as extract_invoice, on every request.
choices.D: Omit tool_choice and tools entirely, relying on prompt instructions to describe the desired JSON output.

explanation:
A. tool_choice: "auto". ❌ Incorrect.
With "auto" the model may choose to respond with plain text instead of calling any tool, which breaks the guarantee of always receiving structured output — exactly the failure mode this pipeline needs to avoid.
B. tool_choice: "any". ✅ Correct.
"any" forces the model to call one of the available tools on every request but leaves the choice of which tool to the model. Since the document type is unknown beforehand, this guarantees a structured tool call while still letting the model select the schema (invoice, receipt, or purchase order) that actually matches the content.
C. tool_choice forced to a specific tool such as extract_invoice on every request. ❌ Incorrect.
Forcing a single named tool would apply the invoice schema even to receipts or purchase orders, producing structurally valid but semantically wrong extractions for two-thirds of the document types.
D. Omit tool_choice and tools, relying on prompt instructions alone. ❌ Incorrect.
Without tool definitions, there is no mechanism forcing structured output at all — the model could respond in free text or produce malformed JSON, which is the opposite of the guarantee required here.

question: A team is designing a JSON schema for extracting warranty claim intake forms, which arrive in wildly varying formats (some handwritten, some digital, some missing fields entirely). Two fields are under discussion: "issue_category" (an enum of defect, shipping_damage, wrong_item) and "purchase_date" (which appears on many but not all forms). Which combination of schema design choices best serves this use case?

choices.A: Make issue_category a closed enum with no fallback option, and make purchase_date a required string field so the extraction is always complete.
choices.B: Add an "other" value plus an "issue_category_detail" free-text field to the enum, and make purchase_date nullable/optional rather than required.
choices.C: Make issue_category a free-text string with no enum at all, and make purchase_date required with a default value of the current date when absent.
choices.D: Keep issue_category as a closed enum with no fallback, but make purchase_date nullable/optional.

explanation:
A. Closed enum with no fallback, and purchase_date required. ❌ Incorrect.
A closed enum with no escape value forces every novel issue type into an incorrect existing category, and marking purchase_date required pressures the model to invent a plausible-looking date when the source form has none — fabricating information that isn't actually present.
B. "other" plus a free-text detail field, and purchase_date nullable/optional. ✅ Correct.
The "other" + detail pattern keeps the enum extensible: novel issue types are captured accurately in free text without forcing a false match to an existing category or requiring a schema change. Making purchase_date nullable lets the model return null when the source genuinely lacks that information, instead of fabricating a plausible-sounding value just to satisfy a required field.
C. Free-text issue_category and purchase_date required with a default of the current date. ❌ Incorrect.
Dropping the enum sacrifices the structure and downstream filterability that categorical extraction is meant to provide, and defaulting a missing date to "today" silently fabricates data that misrepresents the source document.
D. Closed enum with no fallback, but purchase_date nullable/optional. ❌ Incorrect.
This fixes the fabrication risk for dates but still leaves issue_category unable to represent novel or edge-case issue types without misclassifying them, since there is no "other" option.

question: A support operations team commits to guaranteeing customers that AI-generated ticket summaries will be delivered within 36 hours of a ticket being created. They plan to use the Message Batches API, which can take up to 24 hours to complete processing after a batch is submitted. Tickets arrive continuously throughout the day and are queued for the next batch submission. What is the maximum interval the team can leave between successive batch submissions while still guaranteeing the 36-hour deadline for every ticket?

choices.A: Every 36 hours, since that matches the SLA window directly.
choices.B: Every 24 hours, since that matches the batch's maximum processing time.
choices.C: Every 12 hours, so that a ticket's worst-case wait for the next submission plus the batch's maximum processing time never exceeds 36 hours.
choices.D: Every 6 hours, to leave a safety margin beyond what the math requires.

explanation:
A. Every 36 hours. ❌ Incorrect.
A ticket arriving right after a submission would wait almost 36 hours just for the next batch to be submitted, then up to another 24 hours for processing — far exceeding the deadline.
B. Every 24 hours. ❌ Incorrect.
A ticket arriving immediately after a submission waits up to 24 hours for the next submission, then up to another 24 hours for processing, totaling up to 48 hours in the worst case — still over the 36-hour guarantee.
C. Every 12 hours. ✅ Correct.
The worst case is a ticket arriving right after a batch is submitted: it waits up to the full submission interval before being included in the next batch, then up to 24 hours for that batch to process. Setting the interval to 12 hours gives 12 + 24 = 36 hours in the worst case, exactly meeting the SLA with no room to spare, which is the maximum interval that still guarantees the deadline.
D. Every 6 hours. ❌ Incorrect.
This meets the SLA (6 + 24 = 30 ≤ 36) but is not the maximum interval that satisfies the guarantee — it submits batches unnecessarily often (with unnecessary cost/overhead) beyond what the 36-hour constraint requires.

question: A legal team submits a Message Batch of 8,000 scanned multi-page contracts for clause extraction, with each request's custom_id set to the contract's document ID. When results come back, 210 requests show a completed status but their extracted JSON is truncated mid-object and fails schema validation, tracing back to the response hitting the max_tokens limit on unusually long contracts. What is the most appropriate way to handle this?

choices.A: Resubmit the entire batch of 8,000 documents with a higher max_tokens value to be safe.
choices.B: Discard the 210 truncated contracts from the dataset entirely, since they are outliers.
choices.C: Identify the 210 affected custom_ids from the truncated responses, split just those long contracts into smaller page-range chunks, and resubmit only those chunks as a new, smaller batch.
choices.D: Switch the entire pipeline to synchronous requests instead of batch requests to avoid truncation.

explanation:
A. Resubmit the entire batch of 8,000 documents with a higher max_tokens value. ❌ Incorrect.
The other 7,790 documents completed successfully; reprocessing all of them wastes the batch discount already captured and unnecessarily duplicates cost and processing time for requests that didn't fail.
B. Discard the 210 truncated contracts entirely. ❌ Incorrect.
These are real contracts requiring clause extraction, not noise to be dropped; silently discarding them means the legal team is missing analysis for a meaningful subset of their documents with no indication of the gap.
C. Identify the 210 custom_ids, chunk just those long contracts by page range, and resubmit only those chunks as a new batch. ✅ Correct.
custom_id lets you precisely correlate which requests failed, so only those need rework. Since the failure is a max_tokens truncation on unusually long contracts, splitting those specific documents into smaller page-range chunks addresses the root cause (each chunk's expected output fits within the token limit) while keeping the resubmission batch small and inexpensive.
D. Switch the entire pipeline to synchronous requests. ❌ Incorrect.
Switching methods doesn't fix the root cause — max_tokens truncation happens independent of whether the request is synchronous or batched — and it forfeits the batch API's cost savings for the 97%+ of documents that were already processing successfully.

question: A team plans to run clause-risk classification over 60,000 procurement contracts using the Message Batches API. Before submitting the full volume, what is the best practice to maximize first-pass success and minimize the cost and time of resubmissions?

choices.A: Submit the full 60,000-document batch immediately, since the Batches API is designed to handle large volumes without prior testing.
choices.B: Run the current prompt against a representative sample of a few hundred documents, evaluate the results, refine the prompt to fix systematic errors, and only then submit the full batch.
choices.C: Split the 60,000 documents into ten batches of 6,000 and submit all ten simultaneously to parallelize the work.
choices.D: Submit the full batch with a lower temperature setting so results are more consistent on the first pass.

explanation:
A. Submit the full 60,000-document batch immediately. ❌ Incorrect.
Any systematic flaw in the prompt — a misunderstood category, an edge case not accounted for — would manifest across all 60,000 documents, requiring an expensive full-scale resubmission after the fact to fix a problem that a small test would have surfaced first.
B. Test the prompt on a representative sample first, refine it based on observed errors, then submit the full batch. ✅ Correct.
Running a sample first surfaces systematic issues (misclassification patterns, format mismatches, ambiguous cases the prompt doesn't handle) while they're cheap and fast to diagnose. Refining the prompt before scaling up maximizes the first-pass success rate on the full batch, minimizing costly resubmissions of failed or incorrect results at volume.
C. Split into ten parallel batches of 6,000 without prior testing. ❌ Incorrect.
Parallelizing submission addresses throughput, not correctness; an unrefined prompt would still produce the same systematic errors across all ten batches simultaneously, just faster.
D. Submit the full batch with a lower temperature setting. ❌ Incorrect.
Lower temperature makes outputs more deterministic but does not fix an underlying prompt that misunderstands the task or its edge cases; a consistently wrong answer is still wrong.

question: A team uses Claude to draft customer-facing policy summary documents from internal legal source text. To catch errors, they instruct the same Claude session that generated a summary to "review your draft carefully for any missed disclosure requirements before finalizing." In practice, this self-review consistently fails to catch summaries that omit a required cancellation-rights disclosure, even though a completely fresh Claude conversation given only the source text and the draft summary (with no visibility into the generation reasoning) reliably flags the same omission. What best explains this difference?

choices.A: The self-review instruction was worded too politely and needs to explicitly threaten a penalty for missed disclosures.
choices.B: The self-reviewing instance retains the reasoning context from generation, which anchors it toward its own prior conclusions, whereas an independent instance with no generation context evaluates the draft against the source with no such anchoring.
choices.C: Extended thinking was disabled during generation, so the model didn't have enough reasoning budget to catch the omission the first time.
choices.D: The fresh instance simply used a larger context window, allowing it to see more of the source document.

explanation:
A. The instruction needs to threaten a penalty for missed disclosures. ❌ Incorrect.
Threatening or emphatic language doesn't change the fundamental mechanism causing the miss; the same session still carries forward the reasoning and assumptions it used while drafting, which continues to bias its self-review regardless of how sternly it's asked to check.
B. The self-reviewing instance retains generation reasoning context, anchoring it toward its own conclusions, while an independent instance has no such anchoring. ✅ Correct.
When the same conversation both generates and reviews a document, it tends to re-confirm the assumptions and reasoning path it already committed to during generation, making it less likely to notice an omission it didn't flag the first time. A separate Claude instance, given only the source and the draft with no memory of how the draft was produced, evaluates the summary purely on its own merits against the source, catching gaps the generator's own reasoning talked itself past.
C. Extended thinking was disabled during generation. ❌ Incorrect.
The question doesn't indicate a reasoning-budget limitation, and even models with extended thinking enabled exhibit the same self-review blind spot because the issue is retained context and anchoring, not depth of reasoning per se.
D. The fresh instance used a larger context window. ❌ Incorrect.
Both instances are given the same source text and draft; context window size is not the distinguishing factor — the key difference is the absence of the generation reasoning trail in the fresh instance.

question: A content moderation team has Claude flag potentially policy-violating posts using a structured output tool. They want to improve the system over time by understanding which specific language patterns most often lead moderators to dismiss a flag as a false positive. Which schema design addition best enables this kind of systematic analysis over time?

choices.A: Add a single boolean field "is_violation" so moderators can quickly approve or reject each flag.
choices.B: Add a "detected_pattern" field that records the specific phrase, tone marker, or content signal that triggered the flag, alongside the violation classification.
choices.C: Increase the confidence_score field's decimal precision from two digits to four digits.
choices.D: Remove the explanation field entirely to reduce token usage per flagged post.

explanation:
A. A single boolean "is_violation" field. ❌ Incorrect.
A bare boolean tells moderators whether the model thinks something is a violation but captures nothing about why, so there's no way to later aggregate dismissed flags by the underlying trigger and identify systematic false-positive patterns.
B. A "detected_pattern" field recording the specific phrase, tone marker, or content signal that triggered the flag. ✅ Correct.
