Logging the concrete trigger behind each flag lets the team later join moderator dismissal decisions against detected_pattern values, revealing which specific language patterns (e.g., a sarcastic idiom repeatedly mistaken for a threat) generate disproportionate false positives. This turns ad hoc dismissals into a systematic feedback loop for refining the moderation prompt or criteria.
C. Increasing confidence_score precision from two to four decimal digits. ❌ Incorrect.
Additional numeric precision on a confidence score doesn't identify what content or pattern caused the flag; it doesn't provide the categorical hook needed to analyze recurring false-positive triggers.
D. Removing the explanation field to reduce token usage. ❌ Incorrect.
Removing explanatory detail moves in the opposite direction of what's needed — it reduces the information available for understanding why a flag fired, making systematic false-positive analysis harder, not easier.

question: A resume screening tool uses Claude to flag "potential concerns" in candidate applications for recruiter review, such as unexplained employment gaps or credential mismatches. The initial prompt says: "Flag anything that seems off, but be conservative and only report high-confidence concerns." Recruiters find the tool misses candidates with clearly fabricated certification numbers while flagging ordinary part-time-to-full-time transitions as "concerning gaps." What is the most effective fix?

choices.A: Instruct the model to "only report concerns you are absolutely certain about, and ignore anything borderline."
choices.B: Replace the vague instruction with an explicit, enumerated set of concern categories and detection rules, such as "flag if a certification number's format doesn't match the issuing body's known pattern" and "do not flag employment status changes (e.g., part-time to full-time) at the same employer."
choices.C: Ask the model to rate its own confidence from 1-10 and only surface concerns rated above 8.
choices.D: Tell the model to compare each resume against a database of "typical" resumes and flag statistical outliers.

explanation:
A. Instruct the model to "only report concerns you are absolutely certain about, and ignore anything borderline". ❌ Incorrect.
This is a rephrasing of the same vague conservatism instruction already in place; it gives the model no concrete definition of what counts as certain versus borderline, so the same pattern of missed fabrications and misplaced flags on normal transitions would likely persist.
B. Replace the vague instruction with explicit, enumerated concern categories and detection rules. ✅ Correct.
Naming concrete, checkable conditions — such as certification-number format mismatches to flag, and specific employment transitions to explicitly exclude — gives the model an unambiguous procedure to follow. This directly targets both failure modes: it defines a concrete rule that would catch fabricated certification numbers, and it explicitly excludes the part-time-to-full-time pattern that was being wrongly flagged.
C. Ask the model to self-rate confidence 1-10 and only surface scores above 8. ❌ Incorrect.
A self-reported numeric confidence score doesn't fix the underlying ambiguity in what counts as a concern; the model can be highly "confident" in an incorrect judgment (like flagging a normal job transition) just as easily as in a correct one, since the score isn't grounded in explicit criteria.
D. Compare resumes against a database of "typical" resumes and flag statistical outliers. ❌ Incorrect.
This introduces an entirely different, unrequested mechanism (statistical outlier detection against an undefined reference database) rather than fixing the actual problem, which is the absence of clear, explicit criteria for what constitutes a genuine concern.

question: An operations agent aggregates 40 store-level performance reports into a single 25,000-token context before writing an executive summary. The critical finding — a 30% inventory shortfall at the flagship store — appears in report #22, buried in the middle of the aggregated input. The agent's summary omits it entirely, while it correctly surfaces findings from reports #1 and #40. What change to how the aggregated input is structured would most directly fix this?

choices.A: Increase the model's context window so all 40 reports fit without truncation
choices.B: Place a synthesized summary of key findings at the very start of the input, followed by the detailed per-store sections under explicit headers
choices.C: Sort the 40 reports alphabetically by store name before aggregating them
choices.D: Ask the agent to re-read the full aggregated input a second time before summarizing

explanation:
A. Increase the model's context window so all 40 reports fit without truncation. ❌ Incorrect.
The reports already fit in context; the failure isn't truncation but the model's reduced attention to content positioned in the middle of a long input, a known 'lost in the middle' effect that a bigger window doesn't fix.
B. Place a synthesized summary of key findings at the very start of the input, followed by the detailed per-store sections under explicit headers. ✅ Correct.
Position-aware ordering puts the highest-value information where attention is strongest (the start) and gives the model explicit navigational structure (headers) to reliably locate supporting detail anywhere in the body, directly counteracting the lost-in-the-middle effect.
C. Sort the 40 reports alphabetically by store name before aggregating them. ❌ Incorrect.
Alphabetical ordering doesn't change where the critical finding falls relative to the middle of the input; the flagship store's report could still land in the degraded-attention zone.
D. Ask the agent to re-read the full aggregated input a second time before summarizing. ❌ Incorrect.
Re-reading the same unstructured, middle-heavy input doesn't reliably counteract the positional attention bias and wastes tokens without addressing the structural cause.

question: An agent is asked to trace how a rate-limiting mechanism is implemented across a large monorepo. It spends many turns opening files, reading configuration, and following call chains across dozens of modules, and the conversation context is approaching its limit before the trace is complete. Which practice best ensures the agent's confirmed findings (e.g., 'the token bucket is initialized in gateway/limiter.go and configured via env var RATE_LIMIT_RPS') survive if the context window fills and older turns are dropped or compacted?

choices.A: Rely on the agent's summarization of the conversation history whenever the context window fills
choices.B: Periodically write confirmed findings to a scratchpad file on disk, keyed by module or topic, so they can be reloaded regardless of what remains in the conversation window
choices.C: Ask the agent to keep restating all prior findings verbatim in every new message to keep them fresh
choices.D: Reduce the number of files the agent is allowed to open so the exploration finishes faster

explanation:
A. Rely on the agent's summarization of the conversation history whenever the context window fills. ❌ Incorrect.
Automatic summarization is lossy and unpredictable about which specifics (file paths, variable names) survive; it is not a reliable persistence mechanism for facts the agent needs later.
B. Periodically write confirmed findings to a scratchpad file on disk, keyed by module or topic, so they can be reloaded regardless of what remains in the conversation window. ✅ Correct.
Externalizing findings to a scratchpad file decouples them from the volatile conversation context; the agent can reload exactly what it needs at any point, which is the standard technique for persisting state across context boundaries during long exploration sessions.
C. Ask the agent to keep restating all prior findings verbatim in every new message to keep them fresh. ❌ Incorrect.
This consumes context budget on repetition rather than freeing it, accelerating the problem it's meant to solve rather than solving it.
D. Reduce the number of files the agent is allowed to open so the exploration finishes faster. ❌ Incorrect.
Artificially limiting exploration risks an incomplete or wrong trace; it doesn't address how to preserve findings that are already gathered.

question: A coordinator agent runs a nightly batch job that personalizes and sends renewal emails to 5,000 customers, dispatching the work across many worker agents. The process crashes after roughly 3,100 emails have been sent when a downstream mail-service outage causes cascading failures. On restart, how should the system determine where to resume without risking duplicate sends or silently skipping customers?

choices.A: Restart the batch from customer 1 and rely on the mail service to silently deduplicate any repeat sends
choices.B: Have each worker export its completion state (customer IDs processed and send status) to a known location as it finishes, and have the coordinator load a manifest of this state on resume to determine exactly which customers still need processing
choices.C: Ask a human operator to estimate how far the job got based on how long it had been running
choices.D: Restart the batch from customer 3,101 based on the last log line printed to the terminal before the crash

explanation:
A. Restart the batch from customer 1 and rely on the mail service to silently deduplicate any repeat sends. ❌ Incorrect.
This risks duplicate renewal emails to thousands of customers and depends on an unverified deduplication capability in a third-party service rather than on the pipeline's own recoverability.
B. Have each worker export its completion state (customer IDs processed and send status) to a known location as it finishes, and have the coordinator load a manifest of this state on resume to determine exactly which customers still need processing. ✅ Correct.
Structured, per-worker state exports plus a coordinator-level manifest give an authoritative, granular record of exactly what succeeded, failed, or was never attempted, allowing precise resumption without duplicate sends or silent gaps — the core pattern for crash recovery in multi-agent pipelines.
C. Ask a human operator to estimate how far the job got based on how long it had been running. ❌ Incorrect.
Time elapsed is a proxy, not a record of actual per-customer outcomes, and can easily be off by hundreds of records given the outage's cascading failures.
D. Restart the batch from customer 3,101 based on the last log line printed to the terminal before the crash. ❌ Incorrect.
A single last-printed log line reflects only the most recently started task, not necessarily the full, ordered set of what actually completed successfully across all concurrent workers.

question: A customer support agent is mid-conversation helping a user reset a forgotten account PIN, a task fully within its documented capability. The customer writes: 'This is ridiculous, just get me a real person right now, I don't want to deal with a bot.' What should the agent do?

choices.A: Attempt the PIN reset anyway since it is within the agent's capability, and only mention escalation if the reset fails
choices.B: Immediately transfer the customer to a human agent, honoring the explicit request without first attempting to resolve the issue itself
choices.C: Explain that the issue is simple and ask the customer to give the bot one more chance before escalating
choices.D: Escalate only after asking the customer three clarifying questions to confirm the request is serious

explanation:
A. Attempt the PIN reset anyway since it is within the agent's capability, and only mention escalation if the reset fails. ❌ Incorrect.
This overrides an explicit, unambiguous request for a human; capability to resolve the issue is not the deciding factor once the customer has explicitly asked for a person.
B. Immediately transfer the customer to a human agent, honoring the explicit request without first attempting to resolve the issue itself. ✅ Correct.
An explicit customer request for a human is one of the clear escalation triggers and should be honored immediately, without additional investigation or attempts to resolve the issue first, regardless of whether the agent could technically handle it.
C. Explain that the issue is simple and ask the customer to give the bot one more chance before escalating. ❌ Incorrect.
This re-litigates a request the customer has already made explicitly and clearly, which is more likely to increase frustration than resolve it.
D. Escalate only after asking the customer three clarifying questions to confirm the request is serious. ❌ Incorrect.
Explicit requests for a human don't require further investigation or confirmation before being honored; adding questions delays the escalation the customer already asked for.

question: A customer emails a billing support agent: 'I've been charged twice for my subscription this month and I'm getting frustrated with this app.' The double charge is a routine, well-documented issue the agent is fully equipped to resolve (verify the duplicate charge, issue a refund). No prior escalation request has been made. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Escalate to a human agent immediately, since the customer expressed frustration and frustration signals the issue is too complex for the agent
choices.B: Ignore the frustration entirely and proceed straight to the refund process without acknowledging the customer's tone
choices.C: Acknowledge the frustration, explain that the agent can resolve the duplicate charge, and proceed to resolve it — escalating only if the customer indicates they still want a human
choices.D: Ask the customer to first confirm in writing that they want the agent (rather than a human) to handle the refund before proceeding

explanation:
A. Escalate to a human agent immediately, since the customer expressed frustration and frustration signals the issue is too complex for the agent. ❌ Incorrect.
Sentiment is not a reliable proxy for task complexity or for an explicit escalation request; this is a routine, well-documented issue the agent can resolve, and escalating on tone alone adds unnecessary delay.
B. Ignore the frustration entirely and proceed straight to the refund process without acknowledging the customer's tone. ❌ Incorrect.
Ignoring expressed frustration risks the customer feeling unheard, which can itself prompt an escalation request; acknowledging tone costs little and improves the interaction.
C. Acknowledge the frustration, explain that the agent can resolve the duplicate charge, and proceed to resolve it — escalating only if the customer indicates they still want a human. ✅ Correct.
When no explicit escalation request has been made and the issue is within the agent's capability, the calibrated response is to acknowledge the emotion, offer to resolve it, and only escalate if the customer reiterates a preference for a human — this avoids both under-escalating a genuine request and over-escalating a resolvable issue.
D. Ask the customer to first confirm in writing that they want the agent (rather than a human) to handle the refund before proceeding. ❌ Incorrect.
This adds friction to a routine, fully-capable resolution path with no indication the customer wants a human; it delays resolution without cause.

question: An insurance claims agent calls a policy lookup tool with the last name 'Nguyen' and date of birth '1990-03-14' to pull up a claimant's file before processing a payout. The tool returns three matching policyholder records with that name and birth date, differing only in policy number and address. What should the agent do?

choices.A: Select the record with the most recently filed claim, since it is statistically most likely to be the active one
choices.B: Merge the three records' data into a single composite profile to ensure no relevant information is missed
choices.C: Ask the customer for an additional identifier, such as the policy number or address on file, to disambiguate which record is theirs before proceeding
choices.D: Proceed using the first record returned by the tool, since lookup tools typically return the best match first

explanation:
A. Select the record with the most recently filed claim, since it is statistically most likely to be the active one. ❌ Incorrect.
This is a heuristic guess with no confirmation it is the correct policyholder, and an incorrect selection on a payout claim risks acting on someone else's policy.
B. Merge the three records' data into a single composite profile to ensure no relevant information is missed. ❌ Incorrect.
Merging distinct individuals' records conflates unrelated people's private policy data and could lead to an even more incorrect and confusing basis for the payout decision.
C. Ask the customer for an additional identifier, such as the policy number or address on file, to disambiguate which record is theirs before proceeding. ✅ Correct.
When a lookup returns multiple plausible matches, the correct approach is to request an additional identifier to disambiguate rather than heuristically guessing, since an incorrect selection on an action like a payout carries real consequences.
D. Proceed using the first record returned by the tool, since lookup tools typically return the best match first. ❌ Incorrect.
Assuming return order implies confidence is unfounded without the tool explicitly ranking or scoring matches, and acting on an unverified guess risks processing the wrong claimant's payout.

question: A team automates extraction of structured fields (vendor name, invoice number, line-item totals, tax amount) from scanned vendor invoices, using a labeled validation set to calibrate field-level confidence scores. After three months in production, invoices flagged below the confidence threshold go to human review, while high-confidence extractions are auto-approved. Aggregate accuracy on auto-approved invoices is 98%. Which additional QA practice would best catch a scenario where accuracy is much lower specifically on a subset of high-confidence invoices, such as those with multi-currency line items?

choices.A: Raise the confidence threshold across all fields uniformly so fewer invoices are auto-approved
choices.B: Recalibrate the confidence model once using the original labeled validation set and stop further review of high-confidence items
choices.C: Draw a stratified random sample of high-confidence extractions, including a deliberate sample from the multi-currency subset, for periodic human audit rather than relying on aggregate accuracy alone
choices.D: Track only overall auto-approval volume per week as a proxy for extraction quality

explanation:
A. Raise the confidence threshold across all fields uniformly so fewer invoices are auto-approved. ❌ Incorrect.
A uniform threshold increase doesn't target the specific failure mode of multi-currency invoices and would needlessly send many correctly-extracted single-currency invoices to review while possibly still missing the segment-specific problem.
B. Recalibrate the confidence model once using the original labeled validation set and stop further review of high-confidence items. ❌ Incorrect.
If the original validation set underrepresented multi-currency invoices, recalibrating against the same set won't reveal or fix that blind spot, and stopping review removes the only mechanism that could catch it.
C. Draw a stratified random sample of high-confidence extractions, including a deliberate sample from the multi-currency subset, for periodic human audit rather than relying on aggregate accuracy alone. ✅ Correct.
Aggregate accuracy of 98% can mask much poorer performance on a specific segment like multi-currency invoices; stratified sampling that deliberately includes that segment is the practice designed to surface such hidden failure modes that overall metrics conceal.
D. Track only overall auto-approval volume per week as a proxy for extraction quality. ❌ Incorrect.
Volume says nothing about correctness and would not reveal that a segment of high-confidence extractions is systematically wrong.

question: A research agent synthesizes findings on a country's unemployment rate from two subagents: one pulls a figure from a national statistics bureau's quarterly release, the other pulls a figure from an independent economic research institute's report. The two figures differ by 1.8 percentage points. Both subagent outputs include a structured field for publication or collection date, and the dates show the bureau's figure is 5 months older than the institute's. What should the synthesizing agent do?

choices.A: Report only the institute's figure as 'the' unemployment rate since it is more recent, and drop the bureau's figure from the synthesis entirely
choices.B: Average the two figures into a single blended unemployment rate to present one clean number to the reader
choices.C: Present both figures with their sources and collection dates, noting the gap may reflect a real change over the 5-month interval rather than treating it as a contradiction requiring a single resolved number
choices.D: Flag the two figures as a contradiction between sources and ask the user to pick which one to believe

explanation:
A. Report only the institute's figure as 'the' unemployment rate since it is more recent, and drop the bureau's figure from the synthesis entirely. ❌ Incorrect.
Dropping the older figure discards legitimate information about the economic trend over time and denies the reader visibility into how the metric has moved.
B. Average the two figures into a single blended unemployment rate to present one clean number to the reader. ❌ Incorrect.
Averaging figures collected 5 months apart from different methodologies fabricates a number that doesn't correspond to any real measurement and obscures the temporal trend.
C. Present both figures with their sources and collection dates, noting the gap may reflect a real change over the 5-month interval rather than treating it as a contradiction requiring a single resolved number. ✅ Correct.
Requiring and surfacing collection dates in structured subagent outputs lets the synthesizing agent recognize that a difference between sources may reflect genuine change over time rather than a contradiction, and presenting both figures with attribution preserves accuracy and context for the reader.
D. Flag the two figures as a contradiction between sources and ask the user to pick which one to believe. ❌ Incorrect.
Treating a time-separated pair of measurements as an unresolved contradiction misreads a likely temporal difference as a conflict, pushing an unnecessary and potentially confusing decision onto the user.

question: Your automated review calls the Claude API for each PR, using tool_use with a report_findings tool that returns a JSON array of finding objects (each with file_path, line_number, severity, category, and description). During testing on a large PR touching 30+ files, the response hits the max_tokens limit and the output is truncated mid-JSON, causing your pipeline's parser to fail. What is the most effective way to handle this?

choices.A: Increase max_tokens to the model's maximum and instruct Claude to keep finding descriptions under 50 words each.
choices.B: Switch from tool_use to prompting Claude to return findings as a markdown list.
choices.C: Split the review into multiple API calls that each analyze a subset of the changed files, then merge the resulting findings arrays.
choices.D: Add retry logic that detects truncated JSON and re-sends the request with instructions to report only critical and high severity findings.

explanation:
C. Split the review into multiple API calls that each analyze a subset of the changed files, then merge the resulting findings arrays. ✅ Correct.
Breaking the PR into smaller batches keeps each JSON response within token limits, preventing truncation and ensuring valid, parseable output.
A. Increase max_tokens to the model's maximum and instruct Claude to keep finding descriptions under 50 words each. ❌ Incorrect.
May reduce truncation but does not guarantee the response will fit.
B. Switch from tool_use to prompting Claude to return findings as a markdown list. ❌ Incorrect.
Loses structured output and still does not solve token limit issues.
D. Add retry logic that detects truncated JSON and re-sends the request with instructions to report only critical and high severity findings. ❌ Incorrect.
Produces incomplete reviews by omitting lower-severity findings.

question: You are setting up a non-interactive automated code review pipeline using Claude Code. You want Claude to analyze a pulled Git diff (git diff) against the main branch and apply a custom set of code review instructions. However, you notice that when you run the pipeline, Claude only looks at the raw diff text itself and completely stops using its file-reading or code navigation tools. As a result, it fails to inspect the broader codebase repository context, which is critical because the diff modifies a core function called by many other external modules. Which change to the CLI invocation will cause Claude to read related files in the repository while still successfully applying your custom review instructions?

choices.A: Replace --system-prompt with --append-system-prompt so your review instructions are added to Claude Code's default prompt instead of overwriting the built-in guidance for using file-reading and code navigation tools.
choices.B: Keep --system-prompt and add --allowedTools "Read, Glob, Grep" so that the non-interactive mode permits file system tools that it otherwise disables.
choices.C: Stop piping the diff via stdin and instead embed the diff contents inside the prompt string, so Claude Code treats the invocation as an agentic session rather than a stream-processing one.
choices.D: Remove --system-prompt entirely and place the review instructions in a CLAUDE.md file at the repo root, since --system-prompt is incompatible with tool use under -p.

explanation:
A. Replace --system-prompt with --append-system-prompt so your review instructions are added to Claude Code's default prompt instead of overwriting the built-in guidance for using file-reading and code navigation tools. ✅ Correct.
--system-prompt replaces Claude Code's default system instructions, which include guidance for using file-reading and code navigation tools. --append-system-prompt preserves those built-in instructions while adding your custom review rules, allowing Claude to inspect related repository files.
B. Keep --system-prompt and add --allowedTools "Read, Glob, Grep". ❌ Incorrect.
Tools may be permitted, but replacing the default system prompt can remove the instructions that tell Claude to use them.
C. Stop piping the diff via stdin and instead embed the diff contents inside the prompt string. ❌ Incorrect.
Changing how the diff is provided does not restore the missing tool-use guidance.
D. Remove --system-prompt entirely and place the review instructions in a CLAUDE.md file at the repo root. ❌ Incorrect.
--system-prompt is not inherently incompatible with tool use; the issue is overwriting the default prompt.

question: Your development team is using Claude Code to automate test generation across a large codebase. However, developers are frequently rejecting the generated test suites because Claude creates a high volume of trivial assertions or tests that merely maximize line coverage without validating meaningful behavioral logic or edge cases. You want to guide Claude to generate high-quality, production-ready tests directly without introducing high latency or modifying the core pipeline script. Which strategy best ensures that high-quality, meaningful tests are generated in the first place?

choices.A: Restrict test generation to directories where historical quality metrics show higher acceptance rates, disabling it for areas where generated tests consistently require heavy editing.
choices.B: Add post-generation coverage analysis that automatically filters out any generated test that doesn't increase line coverage beyond what existing tests provide.
choices.C: Document testing standards in CLAUDE.md including valuable test criteria, available fixtures with intended use cases, and examples distinguishing meaningful behavioral tests from trivial assertions.
choices.D: Implement a two-phase generation where a second Claude call scores each test against quality criteria, filtering out low-scoring tests before presenting results to developers.

explanation:
C. Document testing standards in CLAUDE.md including valuable test criteria, available fixtures with intended use cases, and examples distinguishing meaningful behavioral tests from trivial assertions. ✅ Correct.
CLAUDE.md provides persistent guidance that shapes test generation from the start. By documenting quality standards, fixtures, and examples of meaningful behavioral tests, Claude is more likely to generate production-ready tests that validate logic and edge cases instead of chasing line coverage.
A. Restrict test generation to certain directories. ❌ Incorrect.
Limits where tests are generated but does not improve test quality.
B. Add post-generation coverage analysis. ❌ Incorrect.
Coverage increases do not guarantee meaningful or high-quality tests.
D. Implement a second scoring call. ❌ Incorrect.
Can filter poor tests but adds latency and modifies the pipeline, which the requirements explicitly avoid.

question: A developer uses Claude Code to refactor a function during their development session. Before committing, they ask the same Claude session to review the code for issues. Later, a separate automated CI review catches several bugs that the same-session review missed. What best explains this discrepancy?

choices.A: The CI environment has access to the full codebase context while the local session only sees the current file.
choices.B: The CI review uses a more specific prompt tailored for catching bugs, while the developer's request was too general.
choices.C: Claude retains context about its prior reasoning in the session, making it less likely to question its own decisions.
choices.D: The extended session length caused the context window to fill with conversation history, leaving less room for thorough analysis.

explanation:
C. Claude retains context about its prior reasoning in the session, making it less likely to question its own decisions. ✅ Correct.
When Claude reviews code in the same session where it created or refactored the code, it retains its earlier reasoning and assumptions. This can lead to confirmation bias, making it less likely to critically re-evaluate its own changes. A fresh CI review starts with no prior attachment to those decisions and may identify issues that the same-session review misses.
A. The CI environment has access to the full codebase context while the local session only sees the current file. ❌ Incorrect.
Local sessions can also access the codebase using file-reading and navigation tools; this is not the primary reason for the discrepancy.
B. The CI review uses a more specific prompt tailored for catching bugs. ❌ Incorrect.
Prompt quality can affect results, but the key issue described is reviewing code within the same session that generated it.
D. The extended session length caused the context window to fill with conversation history. ❌ Incorrect.
Long conversations may affect context management, but this is not the main explanation for missing bugs that a fresh review catches.

question: Your automated reviewer uses a single prompt covering security issues, API design, and business logic correctness. Your evaluation suite shows strong recall for API design findings (82%) but poor recall for business logic edge cases in quiz scoring (34%). When you add few-shot examples of logic bugs to the prompt, logic recall improves to 41% but API design recall drops to 68%. How should you address this trade-off to improve detection across both categories?

choices.A: Split the review into separate focused prompts — one for security and API design, another for business logic — each with dedicated examples, then consolidate findings before posting.
choices.B: Replace the few-shot examples with a detailed checklist of specific logic edge cases to verify, such as division-by-zero in score calculations and boundary conditions in grading thresholds.
choices.C: Upgrade to a more capable model tier, since its stronger reasoning will handle both concern types in a single prompt and eliminate the recall trade-off.

explanation:
A. Split the review into separate focused prompts — one for security and API design, another for business logic — each with dedicated examples, then consolidate findings before posting. ✅ Correct.
Combining multiple review objectives in a single prompt creates context competition, where adding examples for one category can reduce performance in another. Separate, focused prompts allow each review type to have its own instructions and examples, improving recall across categories without sacrificing performance.
B. Replace the few-shot examples with a detailed checklist of specific logic edge cases. ❌ Incorrect.
A checklist may help business logic detection but still forces multiple concerns into one prompt and does not eliminate the trade-off.
C. Upgrade to a more capable model tier. ❌ Incorrect.
A stronger model may improve overall performance, but it does not guarantee removal of prompt interference between different review objectives.

question: Your codebase exploration tool stores session IDs to allow engineers to continue investigations across work sessions. An engineer spent an hour yesterday analyzing a legacy authentication module, building context about its architecture and dependencies. They want to continue today. The session ID is valid, but version control shows 3 of the 12 files the agent previously read were modified overnight by a teammate's merge. What approach best balances efficiency and accuracy?

choices.A: Resume the session and immediately have the agent re-read all 12 previously analyzed files.
choices.B: Resume the session without informing the agent about the changed files.
choices.C: Resume the session and inform the agent which specific files changed for targeted re-analysis.
choices.D: Start a fresh session to ensure the agent works with current codebase state without stale assumptions.

explanation:
C. Resume the session and inform the agent which specific files changed for targeted re-analysis. ✅ Correct.
This approach preserves the valuable context built during the previous session while ensuring accuracy by re-analyzing only the files that changed. It balances efficiency and correctness by avoiding unnecessary re-reading of unchanged files and preventing stale assumptions.
A. Resume the session and immediately have the agent re-read all 12 previously analyzed files. ❌ Incorrect.
Ensures freshness but is inefficient because nine files remain unchanged.
B. Resume the session without informing the agent about the changed files. ❌ Incorrect.
Risks relying on outdated assumptions and missing the impact of recent changes.
D. Start a fresh session to ensure the agent works with current codebase state without stale assumptions. ❌ Incorrect.
Guarantees fresh context but discards an hour of useful analysis and is unnecessarily inefficient.

question: An engineer sees an unfamiliar error message "SYNC_CONFLICT: entity version mismatch detected" in production logs but doesn't know which of the 12 services in the codebase generates it. They ask the agent to help locate the source code. What exploration approach will most efficiently find the responsible code?

choices.A: Use Grep to search for distinctive text from the error message (like "SYNC_CONFLICT" or "entity version mismatch"), then Read the matching files to understand context.
choices.B: Use Glob to find files in directories commonly associated with error handling (such as errors/, exceptions/, or handlers/) across services, then Read each matching file.
choices.C: Use Grep to find all files that import the project's error handling module, then Read those files to locate custom error definitions.
choices.D: Read the project's README and service configuration files to understand the architecture, then systematically Read source files in each service directory.

explanation:
A. Use Grep to search for distinctive text from the error message, then Read the matching files to understand context. ✅ Correct.
Searching for unique strings in the error message is the fastest and most direct way to locate the source code that generates the error. Grep quickly identifies matching files, and Read provides the surrounding context to determine which service is responsible.
B. Use Glob to find files in error-related directories. ❌ Incorrect.
Error messages may not be defined in dedicated error directories, making this approach inefficient and incomplete.
C. Use Grep to find all files that import the project's error handling module. ❌ Incorrect.
Many files may import the error module, creating numerous irrelevant matches and increasing investigation time.
D. Read the project's README and service configuration files first. ❌ Incorrect.
Provides architectural context but is a slow, indirect approach for locating a specific error message.

question: You've configured your Claude agent with three MCP servers: one for git operations, one for Jira ticket management, and one for documentation search. When a user asks the agent to "create a branch for JIRA-123 and add documentation links to the ticket," how does the agent access tools across these servers?

choices.A: The agent queries each server sequentially to determine which handles each tool, routing calls based on tool name prefixes.
choices.B: Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously to the agent.
choices.C: You must specify which MCP server to use for each turn, and the agent can only access one server's tools at a time.
choices.D: The agent automatically selects the most relevant server based on the request and loads only that server's tools.

explanation:
B. Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously to the agent. ✅ Correct.
When multiple MCP servers are configured, the agent discovers and loads tools from all connected servers during initialization. It can then use tools across servers in a single request—for example, using the Git server to create a branch, the Jira server to update the ticket, and the Documentation server to retrieve links.
A. The agent queries each server sequentially. ❌ Incorrect.
The agent does not probe servers one by one for every request or rely on tool name prefixes.
C. You must specify which MCP server to use for each turn. ❌ Incorrect.
The agent can access tools from multiple servers simultaneously and does not require manual server selection per turn.
D. The agent automatically selects one server and loads only that server's tools. ❌ Incorrect.
Tools from all configured servers remain available; the agent is not limited to a single server per request.

question: Compliance requires that refunds exceeding $500 must automatically escalate to a human agent — this rule cannot be left to model discretion. Despite clear system prompt instructions, production logs show the agent occasionally processes high-value refunds directly (3% failure rate). How should you achieve guaranteed compliance?

choices.A: Modify the refund tool to return an error with message "Amount exceeds policy limit, please escalate" when the threshold is exceeded.
choices.B: Implement a hook to intercept tool calls when the refund amount exceeds $500, block it, and invoke human escalation.
choices.C: Strengthen the system prompt with emphatic language: "CRITICAL POLICY! Refunds over $500 MUST trigger human escalation. NEVER process these directly!"
choices.D: Add few-shot examples to the prompt showing correct escalation behavior at various refund amounts ($40, $500, $600).

explanation:
B. Implement a hook to intercept tool calls when the refund amount exceeds $500, block it, and invoke human escalation. ✅ Correct.
Compliance requirements need deterministic enforcement, not model judgment. A hook that intercepts refund tool calls and blocks any refund over $500 guarantees that prohibited actions cannot occur and automatically triggers human escalation.
A. Modify the refund tool to return an error message. ❌ Incorrect.
An error message depends on the model interpreting and handling it correctly and does not provide guaranteed enforcement.
C. Strengthen the system prompt. ❌ Incorrect.
Prompts improve behavior but cannot guarantee 100% compliance, as shown by the existing 3% failure rate.
D. Add few-shot examples. ❌ Incorrect.
Examples may improve adherence but remain probabilistic and cannot enforce a hard compliance rule.

question: Production logs reveal inconsistent error handling: when a tool call fails, the agent sometimes retries 5 times (even if the target ID doesn't exist), sometimes escalates immediately (premature for temporary network issues), and sometimes adds a user-friendly explanation (inappropriate when the issue is a backend permission error). Investigation shows the MCP tool returns a uniform error response: {"status": "error", "content": {"type": "Error", "message": "Operation failed."}}. The agent has no way to distinguish between error types. What's the most effective improvement?

choices.A: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors, returning to the agent only after retries are exhausted.
choices.B: Create an analyze_error MCP tool the agent calls after any failure to determine the error category and recommended action.
choices.C: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns and select appropriate responses for each.
choices.D: Enhance error responses with structured metadata. Include error_category (transient/retriable/permission), reason, and a description of what caused the failure.

explanation:
D. Enhance error responses with structured metadata. Include error_category (transient/retriable/permission), reason, and a description of what caused the failure. ✅ Correct.
The current error responses are generic ("Operation failed."), giving the agent no information to distinguish between transient network issues, invalid tool IDs, or permission errors. Structured metadata enables the agent to take the appropriate action—retry, escalate, or inform the user—consistently and correctly.
A. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for all errors. ❌ Incorrect.
Not all errors are retriable. Retrying permission or invalid-tool errors wastes time and can worsen behavior.
B. Create an analyze_error MCP tool. ❌ Incorrect.
Adds extra complexity and latency. The root problem is that the original errors lack sufficient information.
C. Add few-shot examples to the system prompt. ❌ Incorrect.
Examples cannot reliably infer error types from identical, generic error messages.

question: After expanding the agent's MCP tools with delivery-specific capabilities (apply_promo_code, update_delivery_address, reconcile_delivery), the total tool count has grown from 4 to 7. Tool selection accuracy has dropped from 86% to 71%. Log analysis reveals the majority of errors involve the agent selecting between semantically overlapping tools — calling issue_credit when process_refund was correct, and calling check_delivery_status when lookup_order already returns the needed data. Which approach structurally eliminates the semantic overlap identified as the error source?

choices.A: Consolidate semantically overlapping tools – merge issue_credit and process_refund into a single handle_promotions tool with an action parameter, and fold check_delivery_status into lookup_order with an optional include_tracking flag.
choices.B: Enable the tool search tool with defer_loading on the six new tools, keeping the original two always loaded, so the agent dynamically discovers specialized tools only when needed.
choices.C: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct selection for each ambiguous tool, such as showing when issue_credit applies versus when process_refund is appropriate.
choices.D: Split the tools across two sub-agents — a 'financial resolution' agent with issue_credit, process_refund, return_order, and apply_promo_code, and a 'delivery operations' agent with the remaining delivery tools — with a coordinating agent routing between them.

explanation:
A. Consolidate semantically overlapping tools – merge issue_credit and process_refund into a single handle_promotions tool with an action parameter, and fold check_delivery_status into lookup_order with an optional include_tracking flag. ✅ Correct.
The primary issue is semantic overlap between tools. When multiple tools perform similar functions or return overlapping information, the agent struggles to choose correctly. Consolidating overlapping tools into unified interfaces removes ambiguity and structurally improves tool selection accuracy.
B. Enable tool search with defer_loading. ❌ Incorrect.
Reduces the number of simultaneously loaded tools but does not eliminate the underlying semantic overlap.
C. Add few-shot examples. ❌ Incorrect.
Examples may improve selection temporarily, but they do not address the root cause of overlapping tool capabilities.
D. Split tools across two sub-agents. ❌ Incorrect.
Domain separation can help organization, but overlap still exists within domains (for example, issue_credit vs. process_refund), so ambiguity remains.

question: You're implementing the escalation logic for when the agent should call a human. Your team proposes four different approaches for triggering escalation. Which approach will most reliably identify cases that genuinely require human intervention?

choices.A: Instruct the agent to escalate when the customer requests a human, when the issue requires policy exceptions, or when the agent cannot make meaningful progress.
choices.B: Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool failures to resolve the customer's stated issue, ensuring a reasonable attempt before involving a human.
choices.C: Build a rules engine that maps specific issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions, removing the need for model judgment calls.
choices.D: Implement sentiment analysis that monitors for frustration indicators (e.g., all-caps, repeated questions, exclamations) and triggers escalation when the frustration score exceeds a configured threshold.

explanation:
A. Instruct the agent to escalate when the customer requests a human, when the issue requires policy exceptions, or when the agent cannot make meaningful progress. ✅ Correct.
This approach directly identifies situations that genuinely require human intervention. It covers customer preference, requests that need policy exceptions or discretionary decisions, and cases where the agent is unable to make meaningful progress. It provides a balanced and reliable escalation strategy.
B. Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool failures. ❌ Incorrect.
Tool failures do not always mean a human is needed, and some cases require immediate escalation without waiting for multiple failures.
C. Build a rules engine that maps issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions. ❌ Incorrect.
A rigid rules engine is difficult to maintain and cannot capture all situations that require human judgment or customer preference.
D. Implement sentiment analysis that triggers escalation based on frustration indicators. ❌ Incorrect.
Frustration may indicate dissatisfaction, but it is not a reliable indicator that human intervention is necessary.
